<!-- We would love to hear anything on your mind about Lighthouse -->
**Summary**

Hi !

I am trying to get rid of the "Properly image size" but I am facing a wall here. Here are the kind of errors I get:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1198915/97066910-92428000-15f3-11eb-82e7-6a869b56e595.png)

After checking, it appears that I serve a 800px image, the actual displayed size is 362px and as the DPI is 2, so to serve a perfect image I would need to serve 362x2 = 724px images. But as the design is fluid, it depends of course of the screen width.

In my srcset, I have images that range from 300px to 1000px by increment of 100px. The issue is that it seems the tolerance is super small, and I would need to serve much more images (maybe by increment of 20 or 30px) ?

I have two problems with that:

* The document size would grow quite a lot (especially on product listing pages where I can have up to 50 products, so adding that many srcset will likely outgrows the minor win you can have at serving an exact size.
* This will make a pretty poor usage of CDN: we are using Shopify to serve image, and whenever you ask for a given size, it generates on the fly a new copy at each edges. This initial resize can take a bit of time (especially if the original image is super large) which means that by having way too many srcset, a lot more of users will face the initial resizing, and the cache will fill much faster and therefore lead again to more resizing.

I would like to know what is the recommended practice here, and if being too strict here actually does not encourage a bad practice that would like to extremely large document size, and poor usage of CDN.

Thanks :)

comments.body:
Thanks for filing @bakura10! 

This audit could do with a bit of loosening, but a few other things to keep in mind too...

- To avoid explosion of sizes and best utilize a CDN for fluid images it's recommended to select breakpoints that scale with the *area* of the image (aka the file size) rather than with a single dimension. This would probably solve your case too (though currently might get flagged when the image is smaller).
- The document size increase per image typically pales in comparison to the savings on the image (~33KB here). If you're finding individual `<picture>` elements are even just 1KB after gzip, then something is seriously wrong, and they should probably be dynamically generated instead.

For Lighthouse team, inline with file size breakpoint approaches, I think we should ignore up to ~10-20KB of savings even on smaller images to encourage this best practice (if there is picture or srcset already detected, if they didn't use either, then we can keep the lower threshold 😃 ).

@patrickhulce Thanks for the feedback. The GZip compression is a good point, I will try to do some test to check the impact.

For the first one I am not sure to follow you there. We have a fluid design so possibly an infinity of different sizes for the image based on the width of the screen. Do you mean maybe that we should provide fixed sizes in the srcset based on the 4-5 most popular sizes?

> Do you mean maybe that we should provide fixed sizes in the srcset based on the 4-5 most popular sizes?

No, for fluid sized situations such as yours I'm suggesting generating sizes in increments according to the *area* of the image rather than a *single dimension* (you mentioned that they "range from 300px to 1000px by increment of 100px" on its longest dimension). 

Instead have breakpoints for every 0.1 megapixel increase in resolution (width * height, not *just* width or *just* height) or by file size like [the method described in this post](https://cloudinary.com/blog/introducing_intelligent_responsive_image_breakpoints_solutions). If we decide to loosen our thresholds *this* is the sort of strategy we would respect.

> For Lighthouse team, inline with file size breakpoint approaches, I think we should ignore up to ~10-20KB of savings even on smaller images to encourage this best practice (if there is picture or srcset already detected, if they didn't use either, then we can keep the lower threshold 😃 ).

👍

Hello! :-)

Does anyone is working to fix this problem?
I have exactly the same issue as @bakura10. In my case, it's with "Moto G4", and DPI is 3 in Chrome's Lighthouse (or 2.625 for some strange reasons: https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse/blob/master/lighthouse-core/config/constants.js#L60-L63 and it looks like a hack: https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse/issues/10741 ).

I believe I have the bandwidth to take this on, just looking for some clarification on the intent of the potential fix.

The intent as I understand it, is to allow the audit to pass even if the wasted bytes exceed the `IGNORE_THRESHOLD_IN_BYTES` threshold so long as the developer used scrcset in an attempt to pass the audit; but also the image should pass only if the wasted bytes is below a new, sliding threshold that is based on the image size. Is that correct?

> The intent as I understand it, is to allow the audit to pass even if the wasted bytes exceed the IGNORE_THRESHOLD_IN_BYTES threshold so long as the developer used scrcset in an attempt to pass the audit; but also the image should pass only if the wasted bytes is below a new, sliding threshold that is based on the image size. Is that correct?

Almost–although we want to encourage breakpoints by image area (not width or height), we weren't thinking of making that explicit in the audit. By introducing a `IGNORE_THRESHOLD_IN_BYTES_BREAKPOINTS_PRESENT` (which is ~10-20KB as @patrickhulce suggested), the audit would fail less often for folks whose breakpoints are based on area (which means that the delta between each image would be larger than if doing by just one dimension).

So no sliding threshold. Just a different threshold, if srcset/was in a picture element (these properties are already on the ImageElement artifact).

> To avoid explosion of sizes and best utilize a CDN for fluid images it's recommended to select breakpoints that scale with the area of the image (aka the file size) rather than with a single dimension.

I couldn't find any reference to this in our [docs](https://web.dev/uses-responsive-images/), so we should probably add one. Anyone know the origin of this "best practice"?

> To avoid explosion of sizes and best utilize a CDN for fluid images it's recommended to select breakpoints that scale with the area of the image (aka the file size) rather than with a single dimension.
> 
> I couldn't find any reference to this in our [docs](https://web.dev/uses-responsive-images/), so we should probably add one. Anyone know the origin of this "best practice"?

I checked in https://web.dev/uses-responsive-images/ and https://web.dev/serve-responsive-images/ and see no mention of such Image File Size Breakpoints or how to create them. An official documentation outlining the technique and how to achieve a good test result would be super nice to have.

I did not write any follow up on this but I am still having a lot of reports on PageSpeed for this, without any clear way on how to solve the issue. We try to limit the number of srcset image we generate, we tried a lot of different sizes/steps, but those issues always happen. It would be very nice to have some example algorithm on how to solve this.

I second this and raise it 100%. The report does not make any sense. Even with perfect srcset and sizes this thing flags certain images. There is somewhere in official documentation that I dug up" If the rendered/displayed image is at least 4 KB smaller than the actual image size, it will be flagged as improperly sized". I raise you with this screenshot.

![buggedBehaviour](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36931660/204921929-6313551c-f27f-459b-b4da-5f41aef6749b.jpg)

Is this 4kib smaller ? I investigated this in depth gents you have bugs.  I created my own linter that checks all of the images and all of the properties I assign inside srcset and sizes and even does custom calculations in the background if sizes are incorrect and checks from 165px width to 3000px width. And we developers even when we do a good job or excellent job get questions hey why even though the desktop score is 99 why is this 0.67sec showing there we should fix this this is slowing our website down. Yea right how do I explain this to a client this absolutely has no performance impact??? Additionally besides that  I found other bugs which pretty much means that everyone and by that I mean clients respect lighthouse and think oh its 100% accurate but the inconsistency of the tool is horrible. And we all know that by that I mean contributors, google, developers and etc.
Hey! Can I work on this issue?

Hello, I would like to do a follow-up on this as I really feel there is a problem in this report. You can try this page: https://prestige-theme-allure.myshopify.com/ I get this error from Lighthouse: The issue happens for this image: However the size is correct. I give the browsers images up to 1600w, and the image rendered on my MacBook as 830px. Considering the 2 screen density, the best image would be an image of 1660px, but as I don't have such an image, the browser picks the best possible match, so the 1600px image (which is actually smaller than the optimal size). There is definitely no way I could size it better and I don't understand why Lighthouse reports this error here. @patrickhulce can you please explain me what is the error here?

I have a similar issue here. https://davidmorrison.ch/ https://ausi.github.io/respimagelint/ reports the `sizes` and `srcset` to be correct. ![image](https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse/assets/34193243/ee8602a3-012e-4ced-b841-555518f8cf9c) However PageSpeed for Desktop flags it. ![image](https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse/assets/34193243/989e12f3-0999-4971-a28e-342fb5e649dc) The `sizes` is `(min-width: 2140px) 1984px, calc(94.29vw - 15px)` and this is the HTML with the `srcset`. ```html ``` PageSpeed does not flag it on Mobile though, so there the rules for "Properly size images" are met. Does anyone know what could possibly cause this ? https://pagespeed.web.dev/analysis/https-davidmorrison-ch/aw29646ovc?form_factor=desktop

I second this. I'm also quite confused by the report. Please also note that it's just not viable for everyone to use a CDN which creates any resolution on the fly. Even a CDN at some points need to cache all those generated images. And not everyone can create an arbitrary number of breakpoints either. IMHO that's too strict.  We currently serve up to 8 different breakpoints. If someone tells me that 12 or 16 breakpoints are better - so be it. Unfortunately, at the moment PSI doesn't even tell me how the supposed savings come to be. If at the very least it showed which image for which specific window size it'd deem more suitable, things would become much clearer.

BTW: Does it really respect the retina resolution? We are currently serving e.g. a 900px image for a full-width 450px window size to get  decent quality.

Another related thing PSI gets wrong in my opinion: If we are say somewhere in the middle of a range associated with a certain breakpoint, we should always serve a size according to the upper end of the range. The docs claim we should never serve more pixels than necessary because it's a waste of bandwidth. But if we don't serve perfectly sized images via CDN, we'll always have the problem that we don't want to scale up to avoid quality problems. In summary, I think the policy should be relaxed/resonsidered. Right now it's not really in line with what's actually practical.
Hi, I would like to work on this issue.

That seems to be resolve with Lighthouse 13.0.2 in Brave browser and Lighthouse 13.1.0 in Chrome Canary.  Lighthouse 13.0.1 (Pagespeed Insights) still shows messages where the image is "too large". So let's hope, that Pagespeed Insight updates Lighthouse soon.

body:
[`navigator.scheduling.isInputPending`](https://wicg.github.io/is-input-pending/) is shipping by default in current Chrome stable (m87). Without user input, any page using `isInputPending` has a strong reason to believe yielding during long tasks won't improve user experience so it could keep on chugging instead.

This may have a large impact on any metric looking at long tasks (TBT, TTI) compared to what a real user would experience when loading the same page.

- There was a discussion in the WICG about how it should interact with the Long Tasks API and warnings against long tasks, as well if there should be a way to tell if `isInputPending` was called during a particular long task: https://github.com/WICG/is-input-pending/issues/37
- [Chrome status entry](https://chromestatus.com/feature/5719830432841728)

comments.body: There was some good discussion about this at TPAC too. My primary concern with checking if it was called is that we need to be sure *how often* it's called. I imagine we'll need to do some sort of shimming like we do with rIC.

the last comment in that thread (https://github.com/WICG/is-input-pending/issues/37#issuecomment-698617998) brings up the gameability problem with it, too. Calling `isInputPending` would essentially bless a long task as fine even if the page had no intention of acting responsibly regardless of the `isInputPending` result.

We think an approach like "flip on isInputPending in certain circumstances" could make sense and be more fair to pages using this. Possibilities, always flip on (during a task) at 5000ms since navigation. or flip on after XXms in a long task.


Next step:

* find out how to control the value of `isInputPending()`
* find a working/production app/page using this
* explore what diff emulation approaches make sense. perhaps talk to framework-side contact to determine what a fair/deterministic 
* test impact on long-task metrics like TBT and TTI


mostly chillin for now. 

we're happy to hear from people familiar with `isInputPending()` use in frameworks, etc. "good first issue" if this is you.
Auto-closing: linked PR #16935 was closed (CI checks failed). Sorry for the inconvenience.

title: handling impact of isInputPending on long tasks metrics?

#### Provide the steps to reproduce
1. Run LH on https://output.jsbin.com/vulerujohi for just the `canonical` audit
  
<!-- If your page is only local, or is liable to change, consider uploading a repro so that we can more easily debug the problem. Some services that will help are: https://jsbin.com/, https://surge.sh/ -->

#### What is the current behavior?
The audit fails with explanation `Relative URL (https:// example.com)`

#### What is the expected behavior?
The audit fails with explanation `Invalid URL (https:// example.com)`

#### Environment Information
* Affected Channels: CLI, Node, DevTools <!-- CLI, Node, Extension, DevTools -->
* Lighthouse version: 7.0.1
* Chrome version: 88.0.4324.96
* Node.js version: 14.15.4
* Operating System: macOS Mojave 10.14.6

comments.body:
The example canonical URL `https:// example.com` comes straight from [this audit's unit tests][1]. Stepping through the code, it seems like [the logic for detecting what's wrong with the canonical URL][2] doesn't work correctly. For this page's `link` tag, the extracted values of `hrefRaw` and `href` are `'https:// example.com'` and `'https://%20example.com/'`, respectively.

I'm wondering if this was caused by a change in Chrome's link parsing logic at some point.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse/blob/36e666c1160dab60c37ac7da028d034caafdcd2b/lighthouse-core/test/audits/seo/canonical-test.js#L76
[2]: https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse/blob/36e666c1160dab60c37ac7da028d034caafdcd2b/lighthouse-core/audits/seo/canonical.js#L89-L107

Thanks for filing with such great research ahead of time @tkindy! I think the unit tests are just wrong here. I don't see any tests where we checked that Chrome was actually returning `null` for that URL and `document.querySelector('link[rel=canonical]').href` never returned `null` in my recent exploration, no matter how invalid the URL used was. I think we wrote the unit test without double checking that situation was possible. This appears to match the discrepancy in node's `URL` implementation with the browser's as well.

`new URL('https:// example.com')` in node will throw while in the browser it simply URL-encodes the host and successfully returns a (still invalid) URL object.

You're right we'll need to update the logic for "Invalid URL" and "Relative URL" in this audit 👍 For future implementors, I'm thinking the new invalid check should be "URL is invalid when invoked with `new URL(hrefRaw, 'https://example.com')` and the new relative check is "URL is valid when invoked with `new URL(hrefRaw, 'https://example.com)` but invalid with `new URL(hrefRaw)`.

Ah, good call on the difference between Node's `URL` implementation and the browsers'.

As an aside, I did a bit of testing for what browsers consider a valid URL. The latest versions of both Firefox and Chrome interpret an anchor tag's `href` of `"this is a bad URL"` as a path-relative URL with the spaces percent-encoded as one would expect. However, Chrome interprets `"https:// example.com"` by just percent-encoding the space (as @patrickhulce pointed out above) while Firefox seems to ignore it; the text is formatted as a link, but clicking it has no effect. These behaviors extend to the `URL` constructor; Chrome gives back the invalidly-encoded domain while Firefox throws an error.

These differences aren't super pertinent to this issue (the real question is what search engines consider valid URLs, which is harder to know), but I found it interesting how Chrome seems to be more relaxed on URL syntax compared to Firefox and Node.

Hi @tkindy 👋  
I’d like to work on this issue and submit a fix if that’s okay.
@omwanere I'm not a core maintainer of the project, but I'm sure the maintainers would take a look at a PR if you put one together.

It's really annoying when a website needs to have a double tap on links and buttons when the website is being used on a mobile device.

It would be a nice feature for Lighthouse to test if the website has this poor user experience.

Then if the website does make users have to double tap for links and buttons, give a warning message in lighthouse so the developer knows there is this problem in their website.

Maybe have this feature under the Performance section.

comments.body:
Thanks for filing @ayumihamsaki! Are you referring to the use of a `:hover` style [that forces double tap on iOS](https://css-tricks.com/annoying-mobile-double-tap-link-issue/)?

I'm not sure if we could go sofar as to flag all `:hover` styling served to a mobile device, but it's an interesting idea!

Yes I'm talking about that issue, especially on links and buttons! For example, where I touch a link and it gets a hover underline style, but doesn't open the link and you have to touch the link a second time and then it opens the link.

I'm sure you could add some clever logic to work out the problem ones and the ok ones. It would be a useful tool to have in Lighthouse, where it scans just the webpage and flags up the problem ones as a yellow warning (not a major issue) - more like a suggestion.
Perhaps a candidate for the UX audits @egsweeny wherever that list is being tracked?
We don't know if this is still a problem. Someone will need to investigate.
We like this idea if it's still a problem! Good first issue to investigate if anyone out there would like to put in the research :)

title: Docs are missing section on InteractOutside

body:
# 🐛 Bug Report (or maybe feature request?)

When adding an `onCancel` handler to an AlertDialog, it should fire if the dialog is closed via the Escape key.

See here for a repro: https://codesandbox.io/s/adoring-meadow-7cl4c?file=/src/App.js

Open the dialog, click Cancel, dialog will close, you'll see 'Canceled' in the console.
Open the dialog, press Escape key, dialog will close, no 'Canceled' in the console.

comments.body: Yep, I see that. Seems like a bug, but I'll check with the team to make sure it wasn't on purpose. Surprised it didn't come up earlier.

In the meantime though, you should be able to watch onOpenChange on the DialogTrigger.
I looked into why we don't have it, and the short summary is:
`Dialogs` only fire `onDismiss` for the button in the top right. `Dialogs` also have no idea about the `Esc` key, that’s something that `DialogTrigger` adds to it via `Modal` in `renderOverlay`. So we may need to add this to `DialogTrigger`, which would probably mean a different name for the event. Still talking it over with the team, but figured I'd give an update.

Also, there is no `onDismiss` event while using the Dialog component if we click outside the modal or press `Esc`. It doesn't look correct. Some other libraries simplify it using a single callback for that. Not sure about introducing another one.

As I see even using `onOpenState` there is no difference between the Button that calls `close()` and pressing `Esc`. So I can't tell if a user dismissed the dialog or confirmed it without wrapping `onPress` event for buttons.
Looks like `DialogContainer` fires the `onDismiss` event in both cases. So it would be consistent to have `onDismiss` event on `DialogTrigger` that works the same.

title: onCancel should fire in AlertDialog if Escape is pressed

### Provide a general summary of the feature here

Would be very useful to style the Popover overlay the same way as Modal and ModalOverlay.


### 🤔 Expected Behavior?

You should not be able to interact with outside elements while the popover is open.

### 😯 Current Behavior

If there are elements with `zIndex` set, you're able to click them while the popover is still open. That's due to the Popover Overlay not having any `zIndex` which makes it possible to click other elements with `zIndex`.

Either the popover overlay already comes with `zIndex` or an ability to customize the overlay.

### 🔦 Context

We have clickable elements with higher `zIndex` and if you try to close the popover by clicking outside, you can accidentally interact with an element with higher `zIndex` than the overlay.

comments.body: Related to discussion in #5126. This would be nice to have, and since it's non-breaking it doesn't need to be added before RAC GA.

hi @reidbarber , this is a niche blocker for us as well. we have an electron app we use the popovers in, and the overlays need this special style rule applied:

```css
 -webkit-app-region: no-drag;
```

otherwise, window titlebars eat click events and prevent modals from being closed. 

all we need is some way to target the underlay with styles, either by className, rendering a `<Overlay />` element that replaces the built in one, or some other method as a workaround.

I think we could add an API similar to `<ModalOverlay>`, but for popovers. By default, `<Popover>` would include the overlay, but if you want to customize it, you could wrap it in a `<PopoverOverlay>`.
@devongovett That would be ideal!

This is really needed as otherwise Popovers (Menus in our case) inside a Modal Dialog don't overlay the Modal Dialog which uses `z-index` as written in code examples from the documentation.

As a workaround I apply styles to `[data-testid="underlay"]` for now but TBH this feels hacky.

That would also be helpful for our own context menu implementations. There are a few things to consider:
- How to reposition the popover on subsequent right-clicks
- How to block the browser’s context menu on following right clicks outside the popover
- How to access `underlay` without the `[data-testid="underlay"]` hack

Context menus are common among desktop users in professional software such as Linear. From an accessibility perspective, couldn't long-presses  help here?

body:
### Provide a general summary of the feature here

Chrome 120 introduced the concept of a `CloseWatcher` that can replace a `Esc` key listener. A `CloseWatcher` fires when the `Esc` key is pressed but has the bonus of being triggered by 'back' gestures or buttons on Android, 'back' physical buttons on gamepads, and potentially in the future other 'close' gestures like iOS VoiceOver's two-finger scrub "z" gesture. 

Multiple `CloseWatchers` can be created and will stack, and a user gesture will trigger the watcher at the top of the stack (removing it from the stack).

From [the WICG explainer](https://github.com/WICG/close-watcher):

> Currently, web developers have no good way to handle these close requests. This is especially problematic on Android devices, where the back button is the traditional close request. Imagine a user filling in a twenty-field form, with the last item being a custom date picker modal. The user might click the back button hoping to close the date picker, like they would in a native app. But instead, the back button navigates the web page's history tree, likely closing the whole form and losing the filled information. But the problem of how to handle close requests extends across all operating systems; implementing a good experience for users of different browsers, platforms, and accessibility technologies requires a lot of user agent sniffing today.

Is this something that React Aria would be interested in using instead of an `Esc` key listener (in supported browsers) in `useOverlay`? We could fall back to the key listener in browser that don't support `new CloseWatcher()`. I'd be happy to try raising a PR. 

### 🤔 Expected Behavior?

We will get the benefits of the `CloseWatcher` and mobile users will be less likely to suffer data loss or irritation if they use a back gesture that is uncaught and they end up on a different page as a result.

### 😯 Current Behavior

Currently we only dismiss overlays when the `Esc` key is pressed.

### 💁 Possible Solution

See general summary – I would be happy to try adding this via a PR

### 💻 Examples

* https://close-watcher-demo.glitch.me
* https://twitter.com/bramus/status/1730325130033697278
* https://github.com/WICG/close-watcher
* https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/interaction.html#close-requests-and-close-watchers

### 🧢 Your Company/Team

External to Adobe (my team is working on a rebuild of http://shop.coop.co.uk for Co-op in the UK)

comments.body: For completeness: the linked to https://close-watcher-demo.glitch.me/ demo comes with a polyfill.

I think this would be a good update, especially if it gets support for screen readers as you alluded to. Currently we rely on visually hidden dismiss buttons to allow screen reader users to close some of our overlays, but navigating to those in a large list can be problematic. Having built in gesture support to trigger those overlay closes would solve that for us. I'll bring this up to the team to field opinions.

Talked to the team and we are definitely interested in this, but we'd like to get some research done on how the device back buttons on devices work with native elements so we know which ESC key handlers to replace with CloseWatcher (for example overlays seems like a good place but we wouldn't want to replace the ESC key for something like our TableColumnResizer). Let us know if you'd like to help out with this research!

The native html dialog (when modal) and popovers both have the close watcher behaviour. So basically any elements that use escape to "close" (selects, modals, tooltips etc) are prime candidates for a close watcher.

Looking through react aria that would seem to be:

Pickers - Select, Combobox
Overlays - Modal, Popover, Tooltip

Changing the above components should address the use cases for the derivatives such as Date picker which should also have this.
Small update on this: the Base UI Drawer component (still being worked on) has added CloseWatcher support: https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/3680
Would you like to send a PR? It should be pretty straightforward to add. Look in useOverlay.

I've submitted a PR for this: #9818

The implementation adds CloseWatcher support to `useOverlay` with runtime feature detection and a fallback to the existing Escape key handler for unsupported browsers. The `isKeyboardDismissDisabled` prop and overlay stack behavior are preserved.

title: React Aria: Replace Esc key listeners with a CloseWatcher in supported browsers

comments.body: This seems to be because we will only [conditionally render the nested Modal](https://github.com/adobe/react-spectrum/blob/6e57855257f54738418f98c37efae798bf9fb4ad/packages/react-aria-components/src/Modal.tsx#L129-L131) based on its actual tracked isOpen state if you have a wrapping ModalOverlay. Without that wrapping ModalOverlay around the nested Modal, the nested Modal is rendered and gets access to the internal Modal context [here](https://github.com/adobe/react-spectrum/blob/6e57855257f54738418f98c37efae798bf9fb4ad/packages/react-aria-components/src/Modal.tsx#L65-L69) which is setup by the parent Modal [here](https://github.com/adobe/react-spectrum/blob/6e57855257f54738418f98c37efae798bf9fb4ad/packages/react-aria-components/src/Modal.tsx#L179-L183), resulting in every nested Modal rendering its contents [instead of automatically setting up their own ModalOverlay](https://github.com/adobe/react-spectrum/blob/6e57855257f54738418f98c37efae798bf9fb4ad/packages/react-aria-components/src/Modal.tsx#L86-L99). This doesn't feel quite right to me, perhaps it shouldn't be accessing that internal context since it will setup its own ModalOverlay + ModalContents but I might be missing something here. If it turns out that we do require a ModalOverlay around every nested Modal, then we can definitely update the docs accordingly.
Noticed this is still an issue by opening our example in stackblitz and copying the example inside itself, including a reproduction https://stackblitz.com/edit/3vwpr2za?file=src%2FExample.tsx

### Provide a general summary of the feature here

Replace use of `Number.NaN` values in the NumberField component with `null`. This would also enable passing in `null` to the NumberField which was requested in: #5524.

### 🤔 Expected Behavior?

The expected behavior is that one must check for `null` before interacting with values returned by a NumberField. This isn't extra work, because one must *already* call `Number.isNaN()` when handling number values.

Why is `Number.NaN` less desirable? Because:
* TypeScript doesn't error if a developer forgets to check for NaN
* TypeScript can't determine whether a NaN check was already performed (extra unnecessary checks)
* Serializing a NaN to JSON resolves to a `null` value anyway

### 😯 Current Behavior

All values from a NumberField component need to be checked for `Number.isNaN()`. 

### 💁 Possible Solution

_No response_

### 🔦 Context

I observe most of my team members override `onChange` and `value` properties to support `null` instead. This leads me to think that the DX of the NumberField component isn't quite right. Developers don't expect a number to not be a number and TypeScript doesn't warn about it.

### 💻 Examples

_No response_

### 🧢 Your Company/Team

_No response_

### 🕷 Tracking Issue

_No response_

comments.body: Yes, numberfield is a bit weird in this regard. We'll need to see if we can do this without breaking the current code paths. The behavior has been this way for a long time, and if we did away with it, that'd be considered a breaking change, regardless of intent.

+1 to this. I have a controlled `NumberField` component and I was surprised to find that the empty value is `NaN` rather than `null`. React Aria radios and Selects accept a controlled value of `null`. `null` is more pleasant for type checking as typescript has no `NaN` type.

> as typescript has no NaN type

This isn't quite true, the type is `number`, which was chosen initially to reduce the need to declare state for the NumberField
```
function ControlledNumberField() {
  const [value, setValue] = useState(5);
  return <NumberField value={value} onChange={setValue}
}
```
vs if we had done null, then you'd need to declare the state as
```
const [value, setValue] = useState<number | null>(5);
```

That said, everything else uses `null` for no value, so I get that people are used to it.

I was thinking about this some more, I'm not sure how we could make this change without it being breaking. We can't return null from onChange because then the types are breaking for the handler.

It would also be breaking on the State context that we pass along for React Aria Context.

It'd be pretty easy to allow it on the value/defaultValue props, if we see `null` come in, we can just reassign to NaN so that changes inside the useNumberFieldState hook are minimal.

We can't depend on a generic type to enable the new behaviour either, since the logical code wouldn't know about that. We also can't assume that the initial value/defaultValue will be null or NaN, it could change to one of those later in which case we'd initially be emitting the wrong value. We could put it behind a prop, though I don't love that, and I don't have a good name for the prop. Open to possible ideas.

Unfortunately though, I think this is going to need to wait for a major.

Hi, should we expect further discussion on this, or would you prefer a PR at this point? The fact that NumberField doesn’t accept null prevents us from displaying an empty input, forcing us to prefill it with an odd default value instead of letting the user enter their own.

If you can think of a way to do this and have it be backwards compatible, then we welcome a PR.

Otherwise, this statement holds
> Unfortunately though, I think this is going to need to wait for a major.

And you can display an empty input by using `NaN`. You can map `null` to `NaN` outside of the component if you are controlling the value.

title: Use `null` instead of `Number.NaN` in NumberField component

comments.body:
I came here to request / suggest such an example.

I've got two use cases in my service, and it would be good for it to be shown as an example. I worked out what to do with the macros, but it wasn't obvious if you did or didn't need a fieldset, etc.

Some examples of usage:
## DfE Register trainee teachers
![Screenshot 2021-09-28 at 14 50 59](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2204224/135100291-dafaa0db-cde9-4931-91ac-a3da5d962196.png)

![Screenshot 2021-09-28 at 14 51 14](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2204224/135100352-ae7f10a4-cb2f-4485-ad97-40b8a153df3d.png)

## Passport renewals
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2204224/135101063-59b3340a-92cc-4eb9-8724-6ba3eee275e5.png)

And to confirm what @joelanman, it's a very common pattern in the context of consent / agreeing to declarations. Probably not common across checkboxes in general, but for that one context of use it is.

Here's an example from Find Your Business Rates:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/87323164/135102225-dee248bc-5f36-4ae2-aec0-4aa3a5b90956.png)

There's a healthy debate in the [Make a declaration](https://github.com/alphagov/govuk-design-system-backlog/issues/29) backlog thread about whether or not to use a checkbox for declarations.

There’s definitely a few use cases for single checkboxes without fieldsets and legends though.

It might be neat if `govukCheckboxes` contained a collection of `govukCheckbox` items. So a singular checkbox was a thing in it's own right and a fieldset and legend were only valid macro options on collections of them.

I've given this a little thought but I'm rather struggling to think of examples where [inline radios](https://design-system.service.gov.uk/components/radios/#inline-radios) wouldn't be better when compared to a checkbox. 

Radios allow the question to be formatted as a question ("Do you agree to...?", "Do you want to...?") rather than as a statement the user has to agree or disagree with. 

Radios also ensure that the user has read the question and given an explicit response for validation purposes, whereas an empty checkbox could be the result of an oversight

There's something to be said for the familiarity of an "I agree to the terms and conditions" checkbox, but I'm straining to think of places where a checkbox would be considered preferable.

Could we revisit this? It's a very common pattern. Sometimes radios are a better approach, sometimes a single checkbox, it would be up to the designer and researcher to assess for their projects. Agreeing to terms is a good example for it - it often wouldn't make sense to have a 'no' option - you simply wouldn't be able to continue.

Agreeing to terms is a moderately controversial example, as there’s some disagreement about whether a checkbox is even needed for this, or whether you can do something like "By continuing you agree to terms..." (which I’ve had agreed in the past). Legal opinion on this is mixed! Could still be used as an example, with some caveats, but perhaps there’s a more neutral example? How about something like a "this is an estimate" checkbox, as seen [on this DfE service](https://becoming-a-teacher.design-history.education.gov.uk/apply-for-teacher-training/work-history-iteration/): Alternatively another example could be a mailing list opt-in checkbox, or an "I’m happy to be contacted about user research" type checkbox?
Petition parliament has some

I'd probably argue that a continue button that also opts-in to accepting terms is the controversial option - whereas a checkbox is the more traditional. Agreed it would still be good to have an example.

> I'd probably argue that a continue button that also opts-in to accepting terms is the controversial option - whereas a checkbox is the more traditional. Agreed it would still be good to have an example.

Agree - both are controversial! 

Terms and conditions / declaring things are probably best left to a separate pattern, which could go into more detail of the pros and cons. There’s separate backlog issues for that: https://github.com/alphagov/govuk-design-system-backlog/issues/102 /  https://github.com/alphagov/govuk-design-system-backlog/issues/29

"Email me whenever there’s an update about this" feels like a good, neutral example?

## Related documentation

On the [notification banner](https://design-system.service.gov.uk/components/notification-banner/) page:

"Use a notification banner to tell the user about something they need to know about, but that’s not directly related to the page content."

body:
We've had some support queries recently relating to Hint guidance.

Currently there's no search result, and it is only covered on the Text input component, when the guidance is relevant to any input type.

comments.body: @joelanman There is information in the GOV.UK Service Manual too. https://www.gov.uk/service-manual/design/designing-good-questions#add-help-text-where-necessary
@joelanman does this need to be triaged? If so please add a "new" label and "submitted-by-user" label

Some things that came up recently:

* Adding hint text to fieldset component - I asked this. We're using fieldset macro but I couldn't work out how to add hint text.
* Whether hint text should include a full stop some of the time, all of the time, or none of the time.

> * Adding hint text to fieldset component - I asked this. We're using fieldset macro but I couldn't work out how to add hint text.

@edwardhorsford we _could_ provide additional guidance for this, but it's relatively complex so this feels more of a feature request for GOV.UK Frontend to make it easier to do. I've [raised an issue over there on your behalf](https://github.com/alphagov/govuk-frontend/issues/1166).

Currently, the hint text component accepts HTML which is great because it makes the component flexible.

It gets rendered as a `<span>` with the specified HTML inside of it.

If the HTML consists of block level elements then it becomes invalid.

The following points are relevant:

(1) Interfaces that have a lot of content can be a sign that the design is complicated and needs further work.

(2) If we have done the hard work to keep hint text to a minimum, then we might need to look at other ways to present the information.

Option A: Use an interruption page with the content that needs reading. Once read the next page can contain a simple label/legend + input(s).

Option B: Just have a lot of hint text. This way it’s seen in context of the input and provides a comparable experience for screen reader users.

Option C: Allow hint text to contain the Details component which will let users expand content if they need it.

There may be other options but as a start I’d suggest:

1. Adding guidance to clarify our position on this
2. Make the `<span>` a `<div>` to make sure it’s valid when doing Option B or Option C.
In terms of HTML validation we could consider making the hint component render with a div when supplied with HTML (or at all times) since we make it a block level element anyway with CSS.

I don't think I quite realised that you _couldn't_ put block level elements in there. I've not checked my whole app, but it wouldn't surprise me if we'd supplied the hint with two paragraphs. 

Changing to support this, or providing guidance for what to do in situations where you have more content would be great.
Would it be possible to mention details about hint text in the typography section? This seems a logical place people might go to look up details / guidance for hint text.

I've definitely used the Details component within hint text. I used it to give further examples – so the Details summary was "Examples" and contained good and sometimes bad examples of how to fill in the field. This was for an internal tool.

We ran into an issue with the hint text guidance the other day - a colleague asked me if it was ok to use links in hint text, because there was nothing on the textarea guidance to advise against it. I found [the appropriate guidance on the text input page](https://design-system.service.gov.uk/components/text-input/#avoid-links) but it would have been easy to miss. 

I wonder if there's an argument for hint text to be considered a component in its own right. That's what's done for [error messages](https://design-system.service.gov.uk/components/error-message/) and [character counts](https://design-system.service.gov.uk/components/character-count/), for example - like hint text, both of those only exist as parts of other components. Individual components that allow hint text could then link to the hint text component guidance, as they do for error messages (for example on the [Radios ](https://design-system.service.gov.uk/components/radios/#error-messages) page)

We've had a support request from the Home Office Style Council that suggested the following changes...

Their first request was this change:

> Hint text should not be longer than a sentence. If you need to write more than 1 sentence, use GOV.UK's paragraph style. Do not use full stops after hint text unless you are updating an existing service that uses full stops inconsistently.

Their second request included more changes:

> ## Hint text
> 
> Hint text gives brief additional information that helps people understand and answer a question or take an action.
> 
> ### When to use hint text
> 
> Hint text should only be used to help a user understand a question, answer a question, or take an action. Only add it when there is a clear user need, and after you have first tried improving the question.
> 
> Hint text can:
> 
> * explain the interface, for example: Select all that apply
> * clarify the question or answer
> * give examples of valid formats
> * give examples that help the user recognise their own situation
> * briefly clarify or define a term used in the question or answer
> * tell the user where they can find or look up the information you’re asking for
> 
> Do not use hint text for important information that a user should not skip, or for explanations longer than one sentence. The GOV.UK Design System has advice on [asking complex questions without using hint text](https://design-system.service.gov.uk/patterns/question-pages/#asking-complex-questions-without-using-hint-text).
> 
> ### Keep it short
> 
> Hint text should be a few words only, and a maximum of one sentence.
> 
> If you find yourself needing to write more, it could be because the question is needs to be clarified, improved, or split into more than one question; or you need an explanatory paragraph, which is styled like a normal paragraph.
> 
> ### Text only
> 
> Don’t use hint text as a container for other elements like links or details components. If you need to include these elements, you are probably not writing hint text anyway.
> 
> ### End hint text consistently
> 
> You must be consistent in your use of full stops in a product.
> 
> If you are writing hint text in, ensure it is coded correctly. If the hint text is coded correctly, you do not need to use full stops.
> 
> If you are working on a component for a product that uses full stops in hint text inconsistently, err on the side of using full stops.

"coded correctly" had the style of a link but the link was missing.

comments.body: Focusability is pretty all over the place for those controls in browsers though, I think we need to do some testing to determine support.

To expand on what I meant by testing here: the default controls in HTML5 video and audio aren't consistently focusable, it depends on the browser. And I'm pretty sure it will also depend on whether the `controls` attribute is present. We need to validate whether the video and audio element controls are focusable in every browser. So far they are in Chrome, Safari and Firefox but the behavior is super inconsistent in regards to the experience and number of tab stops (thanks, user agent shadow DOM!). We should check IE11 and Edge to see if they are focusable there.

This is particularly relevant for a case like this: ```html
``` That should probably fail, whereas without the controls it shouldn't.

@namansancheti Thanks for wanting to take this one. For now I'd have to do some extensive testing to see when controls are and are not focusable, especially with Wilco's example above where the video is inside an `aria-hidden` element. I'm not sure how soon I can do that testing but I'll try to get it prioritized so we can get back to you with details on how we want to fix it.

@ljharb the post has been updated in 2020, it's clearly stated in the title and throughout the post. also here's a test using MacOS + VoiceOver and Windows Edge + Narrator (it's what i have access to at the moment) to show the inconsistency across browsers and screen readers with the `title` attribute. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/abb24e22-fb59-4182-a202-792efe1dfcb1 https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/66385ec8-5692-4704-bf82-63f8768210c9 ```html ``` both pages have the same code, just different browsers (chrome and safari). `title` does not behave like `aria-label`, it doesn't always set a link's (or any interactive element) accessible name, certain markup can throw it off. this is just one of the problems of the `title` attribute. i understand this rule is about not having empty links, but i also don't think we should encourage something that's problematic.
More than happy to work on this ;)
Could you assign me to this one ?

@mikemai2awesome do you think it would make sense to have a rule to discourage usage of title attribute for good outside of iframe and the other few [valid use cases](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Global_attributes/title)?
@smellai i think that makes a lot of sense.

body:
[`lang`](https://github.com/jsx-eslint/eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y/blob/main/docs/rules/lang.md) rule checks that a `lang` attribute on the `<html>` is a valid IETF's BCP 47 language tag and doesn't check whether `<html>` has `lang` attribute or not, but the document says `<html>` fails for this rule.

[`html-has-lang`](https://github.com/jsx-eslint/eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y/blob/main/docs/rules/html-has-lang.md) document says:
>This rule is largely superseded by the `lang` rule

so should we fix the implementation of `lang` rule to check `html-has-lang` rule in addition to `lang` validation? or should we remove the example of `<html>` from `lang` rule document?

comments.body: We could certainly add an option that fails if there's _no_ `lang` attribute.

That sounds good. If we don't remove `html-has-lang`, this change won't be a breaking change.
Could I create a PR to add an option that reports missing `lang` attribute on `<html>` element for `lang` rule?
That'd be great, thanks.

### Steps to reproduce:
1. Open NVDA's vision settings.
2. Check Make screen black.
3. Uncheck "Play sound when toggling screen curtain".
4. Save settings.
5. Restart NVDA.

### Actual behavior:

When NVDA starts, with the "Play sound" setting disabled, screen curtain is enabled, but no indication is given.

When enabling screen curtain while NVDA is already running with this setting disabled, NVDA reports verbally that the feature has been enabled.

### Expected behavior:

NVDA should report verbally that screen curtain has been enabled when starting up, if screen curtain is enabled and the Play Sound setting is disabled.

### System configuration
#### NVDA installed/portable/running from source:
installed

2019.3beta2

10 version 1903

#### Other information about your system:

comments.body:
Also, if screen curtain activation fails at NVDA startup, the user is not informed whereas he should.

Steps to reproduce:

0. Press Windows+Escape to exit from Windows Magnifier if it was running
1. Open NVDA's vision settings.
2. Check Make screen black.
3. Uncheck "Play sound when toggling screen curtain".
4. Save settings.
5. Quit NVDA
6. Activate Windows Magnifier and color inversion (press Windows+PavNumPlus and then control+shift+I)
7. Start NVDA again

In this case, screen curtain activation will fail (as described in #10545) but the user should be notified that screen curtain activation has failed.

Note: if you try to reproduce many times theses steps, the color inversion command (control+alt+I) should be pressed only the first time, since this setting will be saved automatically.

cc: @feerrenrut is this a candidate for P2? Maybe a key stroke to report the screen curtain status would be useful here as well (i.e. nvda+0 for enabling or disabling the screen curtain). This would be more consistent to what we use elsewhere. I.e. the status of the option "moving focus to focusable elements in browse mode" is also not announced at the start but you can check the status with NVDA+8.
in the case of a key stroke we would not need a reporting at the start of NVDA.

I don't think this should be as high as P2. A user can use the gesture to ensure that screen curtain is enabled by toggling it. That said I think there is certainly room for improvement in the UX for screen curtain. I think that hearing the earcon or speech for enable / disable should be configurable. I think it is unnecessary to have both. I think screen curtain should be improved so that regardless of how it is enabled / disabled the user is notified. I would be happy for a command to be added to explicitly check the current status. I don't think this should have a default gesture. I think it would be pretty quick to achieve these goals.

I would be happy to take a PR for all or any part. Despite requiring a bit of testing, and lots of permutations of state particularly to "ensure the user is notified in a consistent way regardless of how screen curtain is enabled / disabled", I have labelled it as 'good for new dev' since very little knowledge about NVDA itself is necessary to work on this.

@feerrenrut would it ok, if I work on this issue? because of the limitation of prs. If so, could you confirm the following requirements:

- the user should be informed, if the screencurtain is enabled on startup, when the sound is enabled or disabled. 
- the reporting should be consistend in all situations and all possible settings regarding sound settings and screencurtain settings
- it should exist a gesture, which is not assigned default, which reports the status of the screencurtain.

is that correct?
Or is that to much? Have I somethink forgoten?

all the best,
Christopher

Yep, please go ahead. Please don't forget to carefully test all situations with and without "Always show a warning when enabling screencurtin" enabled.
There a few variations, so I'll be looking for a detailed "testing performed" section of the PR.

Hey,
so a short question,
should the warning dialog also display on startup of nvda, if the screencurtain is permantently enabled and the warning dlg is activated? If so, have you an idea how to do this without breaking the rules of python (don't return out of an __init__) and without changing the visualprividers framerwork?
all the best,

While it probably would be safest to warn for every activation when the warning is enabled, it could be a lot of work, and I don't think it currently does this? 

If you were to try to implement that, my suggestion is to investigate doing something like the following:
- move the current `__init__` code to an `_activate` method and an `_activateWithWarning` method 
- then if the warning option is checked, in the `__init__` use `queueHandler.queueFunction(queueHandler.eventQueue, self._activateWithWarning)` or just call `_activate` directly.

@feerrenrut Yeah, it doesn't do that at this moment. But correct me, if I am wrong, this would be result in, that if the user toggles the screencurtain over the gesture, the dialog will also happen. But also if the user for example press 2 times the gesture to jump over the temporaly setting. in this case, I think, it could result in 2 dialogs. And so on. This looks not like a clean solution. Or I don't understand the code right now? I only want, that my first contribution here is a clean one.

> But also if the user for example press 2 times the gesture to jump over the temporaly setting. in this case, I think, it could result in 2 dialogs.

Yes, you'd have to watch out for this problem. I believe the current implementation works around this successfully. One thing that I think will help, is to separate the concept of "enabling" screen curtain from the objects creation. You might also want to be familiar with #11062 which will result in separating the settings modified by gestures and the GUI.

To start with, you could just focus on the core of this issue. A sound / report when screen curtain is enabled / disabled.
@feerrenrut  ok, I ignore then the warn setting for now and only fix the two issues there are reported at 1 and 2. A pr will come soon. I hope this is ok.
@christopherpross, any update?
@JulienCochuyt sorry, I have Forgotten this issue for Long time. I will fix that  now sorry for the inconvenience.

Hey @feerrenrut  and @JulienCochuyt  should I cover also the case, if the screencurtain couldn't be disabled/enabled? If so, should I handle that in the screencurtain or in the visionhandler for all providers? I ask because the idention of this issue is to inform the user in all cases whether the screencurtain is turned off or on.

Would doing it in the vision handler for all providers, cover the case which 
spauned #11847? That issue was caused by the highlighter being turned off 
automatically when it couldn't be started, with no notice to the user.
(The config is changed, so it doesn't try to turn on again, even if that's how 
it was configured originally)
￼well, that will cover it too, if I understand the issue correctly.

Smaller changes are usually easier to get reviewed and merged. If you can see a way to do this iteratively handling each issue, by the third you'll have a good understanding of what is necessary. I'm looking forward to seeing the draft PR. Thanks for your work here @christopherpross

This includes creating or commenting on issues and pull requests. 

Each of the questions and sections below start with multiple hash symbols (#). Place your answers and information on the blank line below each question.
-->

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

1. Have the PC language set to English (Australian)
2. Have NVDA set to enable dialect switching where available
3. Set NVDA to use the OneCore Catherine voice (the voice is included with the Australian speech pack.
4. Read the NV Access web site.

NVDA reads text from the site ("Donate") with Microsoft David, and strings ("out of list", "visited", "link") with Microsoft Catherine.

Log fragment:
IO - speech.speech.speak (13:33:12.548) - MainThread (20260):
Speaking [LangChangeCommand ('en_AU'), 'out of list', 'visited', 'link', LangChangeCommand ('en'), 'Donate']

The site specifies the following in the html tag:
html class="no-js" lang="en"

rather than a specific dialect.

### Describe the solution you'd like

NVDA should not change voices for a parent dialect ("en"), only if it specifies a different dialect, say "en_CA"

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Turning off dialect switching in NVDA's voice settings would solve this.

My feeling though, is that that unless a specific dialect is specified, if a dialect of that language is already being used, there shouldn't be a need to change dialects in a case like this where only the parent language has been specified.

### Additional context

comments.body: I could reproduce this also in other scenarios, so it is definitely related to the nvda+f1 command and the log viewer. I edited the issue title and description.

It seems like this freeze occurs when appending text to the end of the control.
wxWidgets perhaps could make a more time efficient implementation of `AppendText`.
Given the current limitation from wx, the best solution we see is limiting the size of the buffer of log viewer to only the last 1000 lines.

@seanbudd thanks for following up on this. So if I understood your proposal correctly, with that solution the user gets only the last 1000 lines displayed in the log when pressing nvda+f1. Right?
This is a good solution in the short term I think.
If possible, we should push WX to improve this on their end though.
Maybe it is a rare case, but limiting the log viewer would make issues like #16451 heavier to interpret since we would then miss important parts of the log.

That's correct, just a buffer of the last 1000 lines from the log file which contains all of the log.
I think #16451 wouldn't really be affected as we generally refer to the whole log file rather than log viewer snippets. Log viewer is generally most useful IMO for catching the most recent log lines rather than reading the full log file so I don't really see this as being an issue. You can always workaround this by opening the log file in a document reader.

> Log viewer is generally most useful IMO for catching the most recent log lines rather than reading the full log file so I don't really see this as being an issue.

Just to report that this assumption is not true for everyone. Especially for anybody using NVDA Dev & Test Toolbox which implement quick navigation commands in the log viewer (go to next/previous error/warning/io, etc)

> You can always workaround this by opening the log file in a document reader.

True. And NVDA Dev & Test Toolbox quick nav commands can also operate in any editor in any case.

If the log is cut to its last lines in the log viewer, NVDA Dev & Test Toolbox may just need to adapt and provide an alternative viewer or remap the NVDA+f1 command to open the log in an editor; a link with an external log viewer could also be investigated.

Before cropping the log in NVDA's log viewer, I'd like to confirm that the freeze issue is due to the size of the text content in the log viewer. Because in my personal experience, the freeze seems more related to the object for which the properties are listed, rather than the size of the log. Sometimes, with very long logs, the log viewer does not freeze. Of course, there is also the possibility that both factors have a significant impact.

@seanbudd I question the technical conclusion here.

I have just caused NVDA's log to expand to 906,600 plus lines (well over nine hundred thousand lines) for my current session, using `for i in range(1, 400000): log.debug(f"This is bogus log entry {i}.")` (and similar), in the Python console.
This is far longer than I think is likely for the average NVDA session (@Adriani90 can you comment?).

The Log Viewer takes 15 seconds to open on this 6-core, 32 GB RAM Intel I7 laptop.

Initially there is a watchdog timeout when opening the log, but if I terminate watchdog, this is not a problem.

In fact, the log viewer performance when open, is far snappier than the same log opened in Notepad, which has repeated delays and overall sluggishness, even though Win 11 Notepad gets the file open much faster.

I think @CyrilleB79's suggestion about what is going on, might be closer to the truth.
In any case, I also agree with @CyrilleB79 that there are other uses for the log viewer; and I would prefer to see truncating as a last resort rather than a first one.

Can anyone test and confirm if debouncing the refresh of the panel improves anything?
Launcher build from #19967 to test with: https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/actions/runs/24547736924/artifacts/6488964289

I have just had this issue, about 30 second freeze when opening the log. I am running NVDA with add-ons so they may interfer. 
But here is my experience, in case it may provide hints to solve this issue:
* The log contains 22178 line, 5789997 characters
* NVDA is running for less than 2 hours with log level set to debug
* During this less-than-2-hour period, I have been away about 1 hour, with the computer locked and in stand by. I do not know exactly which type of standby, that's the standard Windows 11 one. Also I do not know if standby matters here; I'll try to check next time if the issue appears with no a previous standby.
* If I open the log viewer when it is not already open, the freeze occurs
* If I open it while it is already open as a background window, the logviewer window is raised to foreground with no delay/freeze
* If I open the log with `NVDA+F1` or with NVDA menu -> Tools -> Log viewer makes no difference. Thus the issues does not come from object dev info retrieval.
* Pressing `F5` in the log viewer immediately refreshes its content with no delay / freeze, so probably no link with the refresh function itself.

Only few add-ons are running: Eloquence (not used) and IBMTTS (currently used).

body:
**Reported by clev on 2011-07-23 14:00**
Several new NVDA users ignore the existence of an NVDA documentation and the advantages of reading it. I suggest adding one more sentence to the welcome dialog to try helping a little more on this. Thus, the entire welcome text could be like this:

Welcome to NVDA!\n
Most commands for controlling NVDA require you to hold down the NVDA key while pressing other keys.\n
By default, the numpad insert and main insert keys may both be used as the NVDA key.\n
You can also configure NVDA to use the CapsLock as the NVDA key.\n
Press NVDA+n at any time to activate the NVDA menu.\n
From this menu, you can configure NVDA, get help and access other NVDA functions.\n
For more details about NVDA, please see the NVDA user's guide and the key command refference found in the help sub menu, in this same menu.\n

comments.body:
Comment 2 by jteh on 2011-07-24 06:49
Adding an additional sentence seems superfluous to me, since we say the user can "get help" from the NVDA menu. Why is this not enough? If this does indeed need to be changed, I'd suggest rewording that phrase rather than adding another sentence. I think shorter is better in this case.

Comment 3 by briang1 on 2011-07-24 08:24
Very difficult. We as humans tend to have the attitude, if all else fails, then read the manual, so I somehow doubt  you would get any further by adding things.
There may well be some  milage in flagging up help more when the dialogues are redesigned to allow tabbing etc between the various  items rather than either opening up one at a time as now though.

I actually find nvda quite intuitive at the getting started level. However, this has to be a personal view.
Any opinions on this matter for new NVDA users? @Qchristensen @jcsteh do you still stick by your comment in https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/issues/1690#issuecomment-155291825?

I tend to agree that if the user is inclined to look up help first off, they will, otherwise it probably won't make any difference how much prodding we do at the point of the welcome screen.  I can't think of any better place to come back and suggest it later, unless we put up a dialog on say the 5th start of the program (nothing scientific behind that, just a number, but by then they've hopefully used the program for a few days, got a bit of a feel for it and could then start to make sense of some of the things in the user guide rather than being bombarded on first run).

If changing that sentence on the Welcome diolog, maybe something like:

From this menu, you can access NVDA settings, tools and help including the NVDA User Guide.\n

However #1951  is approached, i.e. whether the Welcome dialog text is rephrased or the Open User Guide button is added, this ticket will be effectively tackled. Thus, I suggest closing this as a duplicate of #1951. @ehollig
I like the idea. Or provide users shortcuts for NVDA-Menu and may be other important commands. This will make it easier for beginnners.

title: Add documentation hints to the welcome dialog

body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

`gui.message.DisplayableError` is a class which uses an `extensionPoint.Action` in an interesting way, to display user-friendly exceptions in a `gui.message.messageBox`.
In fact, it (appears to) call itself a "type of extension point", although given the placement of that docstring, it may refer only to the actual extension point it uses internally, in which case the class itself has no docstring.

There appears to be no documentation for this class, its expected use cases, or examples of its usage.
Nor is it minimally listed in the Extension Points chapter of the Dev Guide.

It appears not to be used outside the Add-on Store currently, though its placement in `gui.message` at least *implies* it is expected to have more general applications.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I propose that whoever wrote this class, should add a section in the Developer Guide, documenting the class, when it is (and isn't) supposed to be used, and a couple example usages.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

body:
This issue describes behavior that is the opposite of the issue mentioned in [dotnet/winforms issue #8395](https://github.com/dotnet/winforms/issues/8395), where the selected item **was announced when the ComboBox style was set to `DropDown`, but not when set to `DropDownList`**. This issue has been addressed and fixed by the PR in [dotnet/winforms PR #8396](https://github.com/dotnet/winforms/pull/8396), which corrected the behavior to ensure that the selected item is announced in both styles.

Given that .NET Framework 4.8 is the last version supporting WinForms, and a significant number of WinForms applications rely on .NET Framework 4.8, I strongly recommend that NVDA addresses and fixes this behavior to ensure compatibility and consistent user experience for these applications.


### Steps to reproduce:
1. Download the provided [project files](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17958532/WindowsFormsApp2.zip).
2. Run the project and click "Change style" button and observe the behavior of the ComboBox.
3. Press the Down Arrow key.

### Actual behavior:
The currently selected item is not announced when pressing the Down Arrow key in the ComboBox, even when the ComboBox style is set to "DropDownList."

### Expected behavior:
NVDA should announce the currently selected item when the Down Arrow key is pressed, regardless of whether the ComboBox style is "DropDown" or "DropDownList."

### NVDA logs, crash dumps and other attachments:
### System configuration
#### NVDA installed/portable/running from source:
Installed

#### NVDA version:
2024.4.1

#### Windows version:
Windows 10 22H2 (AMD64) build 19045.5131

#### Name and version of other software in use when reproducing the issue:
.net Framework4.8

#### Other information about your system:
n/a

### Other questions
#### Does the issue still occur after restarting your computer?
Yes

#### Have you tried any other versions of NVDA? If so, please report their behaviors.
NVDA Alpha can also reproduce

#### If NVDA add-ons are disabled, is your problem still occurring?
Yes

#### Does the issue still occur after you run the COM Registration Fixing Tool in NVDA's tools menu?
Yes

comments.body:
@Adriani90 
Is there already an issue? I searched before creating this issue but found nothing.
No I didn't find one either yet.

cc @SaschaCowley 
@gerald-hartig
Just to confirm, this has already been fixed in WinForms? Is this issue requesting us to fix this in older versions in WinForms? It's unclear what the remaining work is

Hi @seanbudd 

As far as I know, .netFramework4.8 is the last .netFramework version. The new version of .net does not have this problem.
However, .NetFramework4.8.1 seems to only provide security updates, and Microsoft will most likely not provide a fix for this.
[https://dotnet.microsoft.com/en-us/platform/support/policy/dotnet-framework](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/en-us/platform/support/policy/dotnet-framework)
Thanks for explaining @cary-rowen . Closing as won't fix. We don't support legacy software like this, and Microsoft has fixed it in their supported software.

cc @seanbudd 

The new version is .net but not .netFramework. There are many softwares that cannot be migrated from winForm supported by .netFramework to wpf.

.netFramework has not been abandoned by Microsoft, which means that software based on .netFramework is still popular.

I agree  with @cary-rowen , as long as Windows still supports applications created in .net framework, and NVDA still supports Windows 8.1 and Windows 10, there is no reason yet to end support for this particular framework. Reopening.

@cary-rowen you mention here:
>However, .NetFramework4.8.1 seems to only provide security updates, and Microsoft will most likely not provide a fix for this.

This implies that it is no longer under active support. Other additional material that I have read confirms this.
For issues that need to be fixed externally, we close them when they have been fixed in the supported software.
This issue needs to be fixed by Microsoft in netFramework. If the framework is no longer receiving support than there's nothing to be done.

Hi, I think we also need to take .net apps into account as not all apps were migrated to newer .net releases. Therefore, if the later .net releases fixes it, then we might as well contact developers of affected apps in hopes of asking them to use newer technologies if possible. Thanks.

I have to make a point:
Software/tools used by visually impaired users are more likely to be broken and it is more difficult to find equivalent replacements.

I opened this issue because a user contacted me and said that the software he uses to run his store every day is a Winform application developed by .netFramework, and the developer of the application has lost the source code, which means that the software cannot be obtained at all. Maintenance, migration to a new framework is even less possible.
Trying to find an equivalent replacement with good accessibility in a short period of time is not an easy task.
There may be many examples like this.

This is just one example, but I hope it makes us think more.

Following an internal review and alignment with our updated triage policy for legacy issues, we are re-opening this ticket. This is a valid issue, and our policy is to keep such tickets open to acknowledge the problem and encourage community contributions. 

The issue has been labelled `help wanted` and `legacy/community-support`. While the core team's focus remains on modern frameworks, we believe a fix is feasible and would welcome a pull request or an add-on from the community to resolve this.

During our review, the team concluded that a fix within NVDA is likely feasible. The suspected issue is that the control is not firing standard value change events, but this could likely be worked around. A technical investigation from the community to confirm this and develop a prototype would be next steps.

I've created a fix prototype; users experiencing this issue can try this add-on:

https://github.com/cary-rowen/winFormsNet48Fixes/

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Setting up mirrors is still too difficult for the average user at the moment. Users need to manually get the URL of the mirror, which is obviously very tedious.

### Describe the solution you'd like

In https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/pull/17099#issuecomment-2323929685 @josephsl wrote:

> as for the overall design, a text field might be a good starting point. Another approach might be to have a list of predefined URL's and their user-friendly labels, then add an interface to add custom mirrors and a way to specify the preferred address. That way people can change URL's easily (from a list of mirrors) or let NVDA (or the user) fall back to known mirror(s). For inspiration, take a look at Cygwin installer where one can obtain packages from a variety of sources/mirrors.

I think it's a great program.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

### Additional context

comments.body: We support this feature, as long as there are prominent warnings when changing to an unofficial mirror, and when updating to a build downloaded from an unofficial mirror.
Would it be better or worse if mirror urls could be localized like gestures?

@cary-rowen - I think that might be the wrong design pattern - there might be other reasons than locale for changing a mirror. I don't think we ever want to change the default mirror in NVDA, unless NV Access is hosting the server in China, for example. I also think we should have more formal code review of adding mirror than we do for translations.
I think distributing a fork or a config profile with #18772 completed is the only real solution for changing the default mirrors for Chinese users. I don't think NV Access is comfortable setting defaults to services we don't control. A notable exception currently is NVDA remote, but we plan to migrate away from that to our own solution ASAP.
