comments.body:
Welcome @Neluser ,

We are unable to process this issue as it stands.

- To be able to reproduce this issue, we need a sample document. Please either upload a document that exhibits this behaviour here, or email it to info@nvaccess.org and mention this issue.
- Thank you for providing a log file. Unfortunately, we need a [debug log file](https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/wiki/LogFilesAndCrashDumps) of the behaviour. Please set logging level to debug in NVDA's General preferences, reproduce the issue, and upload the resultant log file.

Kind Regards,
NV Access Software Developers

Good day, thanks for your reply , unfortunately I can't give a sample document as it is at a stage when it is still considered confendial .
I attach a new log.
If the new log does not help in any way, the ticket can be closed.
Thank you.

[nvda.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19335430/nvda.log)

Hello, aren't you able to create a minimal example that you would be
able to share?

Good day, I found the cause of the problem. Disabling “NVDAHighlighter” fixed the problem
I found a similar problem in another old document and the solution worked in this case as well.
I attach new logs and also send the document for testing to the mail specified in the previous message.


[Adobe log.TXT](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19363693/Adobe.log.TXT)
[nvda.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/19363692/nvda.log)
@Neluser do you mean unchecking "Enable Highlighting" in NVDA Settings -> Vision?
Yes, that's correct. When "Enable Highlighting" is turned on, Adobe Acrobat crashes while reading certain documents. This issue did not occur in previous versions.

This needs to be reported to Adobe and fixed by them, we need them to debug the crash dump file and let us know what is causing the crash.
Make sure to provide them a [crash dump](https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/wiki/LogFilesAndCrashDumps) of the behaviour.
Hi, I apologize for the long reply, I sent all the dumps and logs to adobe support yesterday, hopefully that will help fix this issue.

Update for this issue:

Given it seems adobe is not going to address this, in the mean time we encourage a PR to NVDA which does the following:

- disables NVDA highlighter while the Adobe reader app module is active to avoid the crash
- document this limitation in the user guide and include a link to report the issue to adobe

I am still experiencing a similar bug (it's not just links but any use of the arrow keys to move between elements eventually causes a crash) and turning off Visual Highlight does seem to stop the crashing but that's not ideal as it's an excellent way to see where you are in the PDF, especially if recording your movements to show others. Is there no possible fix on NVDA's end, to amend the Visual Highlight possibly?

@chogenkamp - no, there is no possible fix. When we request information to perform the highlighting, Adobe reader crashes. Adobe Reader shouldn't crash when receiving such API calls. Please contact Adobe to request a fix.
@ktdid518 - as mentioned several times in this thread, this is an issue with Adobe Reader. Please report it to Adobe.
@seanbudd What about the confirmed (by NVDA) NVDA bug that causes Acrobat to crash when interacting with form elements when Visual Highlight is turned on?

@ktdid518 - as mentioned previously, the crash with visual highlight is a bug with adobe. Can you please explain what you mean by confirmed by NVDA? Adobe Acrobat is crashing as a result of a normal accessibility API call. When NVDA asks Adobe for information so we can create the highlight window, Adobe Acrobat chooses to crash instead of give us this information.

@seanbudd I didn't see anyone reporting the issue regarding form elements creating the crash specifically. Apologies if I missed that when looking through.

My long-standing issue is: Acrobat to crash when interacting with form elements when Visual Highlight is turned on. 

August 25, 2025, after getting absolutely no where and no help from Adobe, I reached out directly to NVDA via email. After some back and forth, I asked if this issue would be fixed and the answer I received was: "Yes hopefully we'll get that fixed sooner rather than later."

From that point on, I have had to leave Visual Highlight off in order to test accessible PDFs. I have disabilities and would find it incredibly helpful to be able to turn Visual Highlight back on. 

The fact that this is a long-standing accessibility issue must be supremely frustrating for native users that need that visual focus and leads me to my next question:

If it's not an NVDA bug, and NVDA is very aware of this issue directly effecting the accessibility that their users rely on: has NVDA communicated with Acrobat to create a solution together? If not, can that be done? Thank you.

>If it's not an NVDA bug, and NVDA is very aware of this issue directly effecting the accessibility that their users rely on: has NVDA communicated with Acrobat to create a solution together? If not, can that be done? Thank you.

Correct, it's not an NVDA bug, and NV Access is aware of this (I am speaking on behalf of NV Access here). NV Access and many community members have reported this issue to Adobe. Please also report the issue, as it is the only way to bring further attention to it to Adobe.

@ktdid518 - I'm sorry that you were misled with a message saying we were working on it. I'm not sure on the origins of this message, but it wasn't accurate. If you are still in contact with Adobe, can you ask them to provide evidence that they are handling the API call correctly, and send it to info@nvaccess.org. We particularly need details on the crash dump, as all crash information is a secret kept by Adobe. Even if NVDA wasn't handling something correctly, we need the crash information from Adobe.

> Please also report the issue, as it is the only way to bring further attention to it to Adobe.

I think it needs to be stated how difficult it is to directly communicate with Adobe about these issues. There is no direct support email or contact form that can be used, I tried both posting in their Acrobat bug report/suggestions site on UserVoice (and got banned for accidental spamming due to the malfunction on their commenting system lol) and posting on their community forum ([see post](https://community.adobe.com/t5/acrobat-discussions/acrobat-pro-crashes-when-using-nvda-with-visual-highlight-setting-turned-on/td-p/15665964)) where I got one reply from an Adobe staff person saying they couldn't replicate the crash and asking me for more app info, which I provided and 5 days later, no new reply from that staff member. I even tried to include a copy of your comment @seanbudd about API for their records. 

The reality is that posting bug reports or posting for help on the Adobe forums only gets us so far, as just regular Adobe customers, we're a dime a dozen, easy to ignore. This ends up being a "broken telephone" situation where we are poorly playing intermediary between NVDA and Adobe, when really the best way to resolve this issue would be for NVDA and Adobe to communicate directly. Is there no direct contact between NVDA and Adobe, no one at NVDA with any sort of direct relationship with a rep at Adobe, in their accessibility department or elsewhere?

I'm not sure what to say @chogenkamp about Adobe's poor customer service here, it's very easy to reproduce this crash. Please remember that "NVDA" is the community; it's you, me and all our volunteers and users. Even when we contact them as NV Access, the organisation, their priorities and business practices are up to them. We try to to also report all serious third party issues with the developers where possible, but unfortunately software developers don't magically listen to us, they still have their own priorities. It often takes intense community pressure and lawsuits, to get software companies to comply with accessibility. These companies are often ignoring their own internal experts on accessibility, advocating for these fixes. Communication from NV Access is just a small drop in the bucket of what is needed to get these things fixed.

@chogenkamp in the link you've provided you write:
> And I didn't change the NVDA settings from the default install settings, which includes showing the Speech Viewer panel on start up and Visual Highlight - Enable Highlighting was turned on by default

That's not true. Visual Highlight is disabled by default in NVDA's settings and, as well, the speech viewer is not showing at NVDA startup by default. If an Adobe employee has tried to reproduce, they may just have started NVDA with its default settings, opened the document and read it, and did not succeed in reproducing.

For whoever is reproducing this issue, I have not tried myself, I'd recommend to open a brand new issue replacing this one, putting in the initial description:
* Detailed steps to reproduce
* a simple sample document where the issue can be reproduced
* updated NVDA, Windows and Acrobat versions

Now that we have understood the cause of this issue, an Adobe employee wanting to investigate this issue should not have to read all the comments to find the sample document or how to configure NVDA to reproduce, the more if Adobe is not easy to make collaborate.

@CyrilleB79 Sorry I did a lot of different things trying to troubleshoot this issue so yes I may have gotten confused what exactly are the default settings for NVDA, I was just basing my remark on how NVDA functioned when I last uninstalled it and reinstalled it to try to fix this problem, when I opened NVDA it showed Visual Highlight and the Speech Viewer, maybe those were my changes that somehow persisted in my laptop even after I uninstalled NVDA. All the more reason for someone from NVDA more knowledgeable with the specifics of the app to work directly with Adobe, it took me literally months to figure out that the issue was in fact Visual Highlight because I am not an app developer who might have the "insider info" to better guess what is happening. I can't overstate how painfully slow it can be to try to figure out what is wrong with an app when you think you've done everything right and there's not an obvious correlation between Action A and the app crashing.

@seanbudd I appreciate what you are saying about it being easy to ignore accessibility, but really that just cements my original point that telling people to report things to Adobe doesn't seem particularly productive as it creates a false sense that a) it's easy to do so (hence why I mentioned my difficulties, Adobe pretends to encourage feedback but realistically makes it very difficult to get in touch with an actual person in a way that Adobe staff just can't ignore) and b) that realistically using the only Adobe-provided means for flagging bugs (either their wonky UserVoice site or the community forums) feels like the chance of getting Adobe staff to even notice let alone do anything, you might as well stand outside and shout your bug to the wind. If future suggestions like that were amended to "Please report it to Adobe, though typically they may not be responsive to reports" that would cover it for me, just a suggestion.

> [@CyrilleB79](https://github.com/CyrilleB79) Sorry I did a lot of different things trying to troubleshoot this issue so yes I may have gotten confused what exactly are the default settings for NVDA, I was just basing my remark on how NVDA functioned when I last uninstalled it and reinstalled it to try to fix this problem, when I opened NVDA it showed Visual Highlight and the Speech Viewer, maybe those were my changes that somehow persisted in my laptop even after I uninstalled NVDA.

Yes, settings are persisted when you uninstall and reinstall NVDA and you are not even offered the option to remove them, unfortunately (see #6869 and #9596).

Can this issue be reproduced in Acrobat Reader? I triggered a few crashes while testing with Acrobat Reader before, but I am unable to do so now.

I'm sorry, I don't have access to Acrobat Pro for testing.

Additionally, this issue is likely related to the virtual buffer. Have you tried disabling only the browse mode highlight?

Locking this thread as the conversation has resolved and we want to ensure the resolution doesn't get lost in further comments.

This issue is caused by NVDA trying to perform visual highlight in Adobe Reader. When we attempt this, Acrobat causes the crash. We cannot work around this or avoid this. Only Adobe can investigate and fix this.
In the mean time, avoid using visual highlight in adobe reader.
It seems adobe is not going to address this, in the mean time we encourage a PR to NVDA which does the following:

* disables NVDA highlighter while the Adobe reader app module is active to avoid the crash
* document this limitation in the user guide and include a link to report the issue to adobe

comments.body: After checking the logs, this is a config issue. We cannot support downgrades, as there is no way for an old copy of NVDA to understand a config from a newer copy of NVDA. See #18772 for a potential solution for handling downgrades in future.

@seanbudd
I wasn't trying to downgrade. A friend of mine asked me for this version because he was using Windows 7. He also wanted some synthesizer plugins, so I had to make a portable version and send it to him. I wanted to make a portable version of 2023.3 for him, but I couldn't get the installer to work. To create a portable version of 2023.3, I had to completely delete the existing NVDA on my computer. Shouldn't this be considered a problem?
I think the best we can do is reset config to the default config when running the installer, and loading the config fails

@seanbudd
I still think this is a problem. If for some reason we want to create a portable NVDA from a previous version, we'll have to reset the settings to the default configuration. Why do installers use the configuration of the installed NVDAs on our computers?

The installer generally uses the same config as the installed copy to improve ease of use, i.e. to retain braille display detection, synthesizer choice, etc.
However, if you are running an older installer than the config you have installed, there is nothing we can logically due to support or process it. We logically can't safely downgrade a config, as an older copy of NVDA cannot be aware of a newer copies config spec. There's physically no way to tell the older copy how to modify the config. 
If you want to create a portable copy from a previous version, you will have to create the config from scratch, as logically it is impossible to use a new config on an old copy of NVDA.

@seanbudd
So, can't we add a setting to the installed NVDAs on our computers to determine whether other installers can use the same configuration? Because, as I mentioned, this causes previous NVDAs to fail to start. We either have to reset the settings of our installed NVDAs to default, as you suggested, or we have to completely uninstall NVDA and run the previous version, as I did. Even trying to run a previous version installer by completely shutting down my installed NVDA and then opening the installer with NVDA closed didn't work.

No, that sort of setting is logically impossible. The only way we can determine if another installer can use the config is by trying to use it. If loading the config fails, we can reset it to the default config while the installer is running. The installer won't save this. This won't disrupt the installed copy, only the temporary installer copy. This fix also will not work for older copies, i.e. there's nothing we can do about 2023.3, but we can make it so downgrading from 2028.1 to 2026.1 works.

@seanbudd
The only thing that will be reset is the installer itself, right? If our installed NVDAs were also reset, that would also disrupt our current settings. But if you're only aiming to reset the installer to its default settings, it would be great if this were implemented. At least this issue would be resolved in future versions.
Yes, only the installer itself. The installer doesn't save any config changes to disk

Yes we could update `InstallingOverNewerVersionDialog` to add a message about config being reset if it is going to be reset after installing. Currently the message is:

> "You are attempting to install an earlier version of NVDA "
> "than the version currently installed. "
> "If you really wish to revert to an earlier version, "
> "you should first cancel this installation "
> "and completely uninstall NVDA before installing the earlier version."

body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

In the built-in Remote Access feature, there doesn't appear to be a way to reestablish the connection configured to auto-connect at startup without restarting NVDA. Before Remote Access was built in, if you were using the NVDA Remote addon, simply reloading plugins would accomplish this.

### Describe the solution you'd like

There should be a simple command, ideally able to be assigned to an input gesture, to close the current Remote Access connection if needed, then reestablish the connection set up when NVDA started. If there's a dialog asking for confirmation to complete the operation, there should ideally be a "Don't ask me this again" checkbox.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

As far as I know, the only solution at this point is to restart NVDA. While this works, it takes longer than the Reload Plugins solution did when NVDA Remote was an addon.

### Additional context

The use case is a situation where you have NVDA set up to allow your system to be controlled by default, but need to temporarily control another machine, and then want to resume the connection allowing your machine to be controlled.

comments.body:
I'm thinking about a question:
Revert to saved configuration and Reset configuration to factory defaults Should the connection be reset?
If the answer is yes, then there may be a risk of accidentally disconnecting other connections, and if the answer is no (i.e., the current behavior), it will give me the feeling that Revert to saved configuration does not fully restore the configuration to its previous state.
Perhaps when the Automatically connect after NVDA starts option is enabled and the configuration is attempted to restore, if it is detected that the current remote access connection is inconsistent with the information saved in the configuration, a dialog box pops up asking the user how to choose will be a more appropriate compromise.

How about an option in the remote access menu, Reload Remote Access Configuration? Or something along those lines. This would then detect any inconsistencies in connection state between the current connection and any automatic connection set up, if it is set. If so, the current connection could be disconnected and the configured connection could be reconnected, without restoring the entire configuration to default.

This might be useful if someone has set up Remote Access to automatically connect, but hasn't yet saved that configuration, so it's only in memory. If, for instance, they wanted to test their configuration without restarting NVDA or saving their configuration. If it isn't accurate or what they wanted, they could then restore their configuration to the last saved configuration and try again.

This menu option could have an unassigned gesture for activating it initially, which could be assigned by the user later, if desired.
What if there was a function to swap the controller and the controlled?

I recently created a new add-on called RemotePlusPlus, which includes the feature needed here, along with some other enhancements. Please see:
[https://github.com/cary-rowen/remotePlusPlus](https://github.com/cary-rowen/remotePlusPlus)

body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Connect an Eurobraille b.note braille display to a Windows PC.
2. Run NVDA 2025.3.2 and configure the b.note as the active braille display.
3. Enter review mode (object or document review).
4. Try to use the left joystick **up** on the b.note to move to the previous line in review.
5. Observe that the b.note internal menu opens instead, and the review cursor in NVDA does **not** move to the previous line.

### Actual behavior

On Eurobraille b.note, pressing the left joystick up opens the internal b.note applications menu (as documented in the official b.note user manual).  
Therefore, the joystick up gesture does not reach NVDA as a navigation key.

In NVDA’s Eurobraille driver, `joystick1Up` is mapped to the `review_previousLine` command.  
Because the firmware reserves joystick up for the menu, b.note users cannot use the default gesture to move to the previous line in review mode.

There is also no secondary default gesture mapped to `review_previousLine` for Eurobraille/b.note, so the only workaround is for users to manually create a custom input gesture in NVDA.

### Expected behavior

NVDA should provide at least one working default gesture on Eurobraille b.note to move to the **previous line in review mode**, using a gesture that is not intercepted by the device firmware (or adjusting the mapping so that joystick up is not assumed to be available on b.note).

### NVDA logs, crash dumps and other attachments

No crashes or exceptions.  
Logs can be provided if needed, but the problem is mainly a mismatch between the NVDA Eurobraille driver mapping and the Eurobraille b.note firmware behavior.

### System configuration

#### NVDA installed/portable/running from source

Installed

#### NVDA version

2025.3.2 (stable)

#### Windows version

Windows 11 64-bit (French)

#### Name and version of other software in use when reproducing the issue

Eurobraille b.note braille display (USB or Bluetooth connection), using the standard b.note firmware and NVDA’s built-in Eurobraille driver.

No other specific software is required to reproduce the issue.

#### Other information about your system

- NVDA language: French
- Braille table: French 6-dot / 8-dot (any table, problem is independent of braille table choice)

### Other questions

#### Does the issue still occur after restarting your computer?

Yes.

#### Have you tried any other versions of NVDA? If so, please report their behaviors

The behavior is reproducible with NVDA 2025.3.2.  
The problem is expected to exist in other recent versions as well, since it comes from the Eurobraille driver mapping.

#### If NVDA add-ons are disabled, is your problem still occurring?

Yes, the issue occurs with all add-ons disabled.

#### Does the issue still occur after you run the COM Registration Fixing Tool in NVDA's tools menu?

Yes, this issue is unrelated to COM registration. It is a mapping mismatch between the Eurobraille b.note firmware and the Eurobraille driver in NVDA.

body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

In #18898, the length of indentation beeps was reduced from 80ms to 40ms. However, this can cause problems for some users, such as those using audio cues in VS Code.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Completing the work started in that PR, expose the config item for indentation done duration in the GUI.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

1. Keep as-is - this breaks a feature for some users.
2. Revert the change - this worsens the UX of this feature for other users.

### Additional context

https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/pull/18898#issuecomment-3604855379

comments.body:
It's worth noting that #18898 does not fix any issue opened in GitHub, or at least it does not appear in the PR description.

Were there really complains of the original beep being too long? Or has it been reduced mainly for personal convenience of the PR's author (@SaschaCowley)? If the latter, I'd recommend to:
* turn back to the original value, because nobody complained of the original value for long while one person already complained of the new value, even only in alpha stage.
* keep it configurable and add a control to configure it in advanced settings panel, so that we can experiment various values with many users.

I am rather against making it a publicly available customizable value, i.e. otherwhere thant in Advanced settings. Else, if we begin like this, we end up with every beep pitch, length, every message, every color, etc. being configurable in NVDA and its option will become a huge collection of unsignificant parameters.

Rather it's up to developers / testers to try to define a value that would suit everybody.

> I am rather against making it a publicly available customizable value, i.e. otherwhere thant in Advanced settings. Else, if we begin like this, we end up with every beep pitch, length, every message, every color, etc. being configurable in NVDA and its option will become a huge collection of unsignificant parameters.

I think this is a rather blatant disregard for how useful configuration options are. Not only does NVDA already expose loads of configuration options that IMO fall under the broad categorization you've given here, look at the document formatting settings, this is an option that:

* Is used by software engineers, even the people who work on NVDA,
* Is something that personally speaking I have heard many of my developer friends speak positively about,
* Was indeed indirectly requested by a user, myself. I and @SaschaCowley both agreed the current beeps were too long, with them saying that the duration is a reason they don't use the feature. I myself use them but always found the duration to be a distraction.
* Another feature related to indent beeps was added not too long ago, "Ignore blank lines for line indentation reporting". Are you also opposed to this being a configurable setting? I've personally speaking never seen anyone use it, but that's not to say they don't exist, they obviously do if an add-on was made and later a pull request to merge it into core. That being said, I don't think there was much pushback on putting this one in the GUI?

@trypsynth, I am sorry that my previous comment has offended you.

I'll try to clarify my point. Initially we had 80 ms beeps, that were found to be distractingly long, especialy at high speech rates by Sascha, you and probably other people (how many, we do not know). Now we have 40 ms beeps, found to be too short by some Visual Studio users (we do not know either how many) due to conflict with audio cues.
I do not question the validity of the problems encountered by all these users.

I just wanted to emphasize the fact that when two groups of users encounter conflicting interests regarding a feature, the easiest solution is to add a configurable option so that each one can configure according to their needs. But maybe other users won't even know that there is an option and will just feel that, for example, indent tone reporting is not for them just because it conflicts with audio cues of Visual Studio.

IMO a better approach is first to see if we cannot determine a way to provide a feature adapted to all users without the need to configure something. And only if it is not possible, fall back to a configurable option.

In our example, have we answered the following questions:
* Could an intermediate value between 40 ms and 80 ms meet the expectations of everyone?
* Would a duration based on the speech rate be doable? And would it be more suitable?
* Is there a way to have NVDA's indent tones and audio cues of Visual Studio played sequentially rather than together? (seems difficult to me but maybe someone has an idea)
* Can we change the waveform or other audio properties of the indent tones so that hearing cues along with them, the user can still hear both?
* etc.

To summarize, we should find a trade-off between having a GUI always more complex (i.e. with always more options) and a software that can meet the needs of a majority of users.

> I just wanted to emphasize the fact that when two groups of users encounter conflicting interests regarding a feature, the easiest solution is to add a configurable option so that each one can configure according to their needs. But maybe other users won't even know that there is an option and will just feel that, for example, indent tone reporting is not for them just because it conflicts with audio cues of Visual Studio.

You're right that we shouldn't just expose configuration options to users for every possible parameter. However, in #18898, I made indent tone duration part of the config spec, as I foresaw this possibility, but we decided not to expose it in the GUI as we weren't sure if it was necessary, and when simple is satisfactory, it is generally better.

> IMO a better approach is first to see if we cannot determine a way to provide a feature adapted to all users without the need to configure something. And only if it is not possible, fall back to a configurable option.

We could change the default to 60ms in an attempt to satisfy everyone, but I suspect that we won't be successful, so people are going to manually edit their `nvda.ini`, or do so via the Python console, both of which are riskier than just exposing this option in the GUI.

> * Would a duration based on the speech rate be doable? And would it be more suitable?

Doable, yes. Suitable? I'm not sure. Especially given the variability of speech rates across synths.

>     * Is there a way to have NVDA's indent tones and audio cues of Visual Studio played sequentially rather than together? (seems difficult to me but maybe someone has an idea)

Not practically, I don't think. We could introduce a delay to the tones in VS Code, but I think this is a bad solution. It would also need to be configurable (not everyone uses the audio cues in VS Code), and IMO this is a more difficult feature to explain than the solution proposed here.

>     * Can we change the waveform or other audio properties of the indent tones so that hearing cues along with them, the user can still hear both?

We definitely could, though it would be a lot more work for unknown benefit.

I think that what I am proposing is a pragmatic solution that is easy to understand (from a user point-of-view) and simple and safe to implement. You have proposed some really interesting ideas that are absolutely worth exploring, but they are all much more complicated than what I am proposing. We also need to be pragmatic. There are only so many hours in the day :)

Thanks @SaschaCowley for elaborating a bit more, that was my main request.

So if we agree that there is no better solution than creating a new option, the question that comes is: where will this option land?

I imagine that it will land in doc formatting settings, after "Line indentation reporting:" or after "Ignore blank lines for line indentation reporting".

If yes, I'd like to raise the point that the Doc formatting settings panel becomes less and less consistent. In the past, it was consisting in a list of checkboxes saying what is reported or not, exception made of the last item ("Report formatting changes after the cursor"). Along with time, we have inserted new items which do not define anymore if something is reported or not but how it is reported if another checkbox is checked ("Ignore blank lines for line indentation reporting", "Link type"). I do not ask to find a solution to this problem before this issue is implemented, but I just want to draw your attention to this point when we give more opportunities to configure how formatting is reported.

labels.name: triaged
labels.description: Has been triaged, issue is waiting for implementation.

title: Make duration of indentation beeps configurable

body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

The display models of other screen readers, such as JAWS and ZDSR, is able to find text NVDA's display model can't find. This makes a lot of applications that doesn't have accessibillity API support unusable with NVDA, but usable with other screen readers. Yes, the developers of those applications should add accessibillity API support, but what if they don't, or the application isn't develloped any more?

applications where NVDA's display model can't find text, but other models can includes

* Applications written with tk/tkinter (JAWS only. Completely unusable with NVDA and ZDSR)
* Pidgin (ZDSR only. Completely unusable with NVDA and JAWS)
* eclipse Java IDE (Most things works with accessibility APIs, but some text doesn't. This text is only accessible with JAWS display model)
* Mysql workbench (Works mostly with accessibility APIs. Some of the text that doesn't is accessible with both the display models in JAWS and ZDSR)

The display models of other screen readers also often support graphicks drawn to the screen. JAWS, in addition to graphics, also supports symbols, such as checkmarks, unchecked checkmarks, dropdown symbols, and so on. This can be useful if the checked/unchecked state isn't exposed through accessibility APIs, such as in the wx.CheckListBox control.
for more context, see issue #15016

### Describe the solution you'd like

Add a second display model to NVDA, that implements graphics support, support for symbols, and better/different text fetching support, so it can find the text that other display models can, but the current display model in NVDA can't. Allow the user to turn off the rendering of graphics and symbols, and allow them to switch between the new display model and the original one, in case the original works in places the new one doesn't. Allow add-ons to add their own display models. I think it is best if this is done via a path stored in the configuration, that isn't configurable by the user. If the path doesn't point to a .dll file, then use the displayModel provided by core

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body:
I know nothing about ZDSR, but Jaws uses video intercept, which allows for this graphics support. It also introduces a great deal more complexity, and I have encountered situations where the video intercept capability of Jaws causes problems, doesn't work, or simply increases CPU use on a system after Jaws is first launched, whether or not it continues to be used after first launch.

I can see the advantages in this feature, but the disadvantages should also be considered, the most significant of which, in my opinion, would be a component that would be far more complex than existing accessibility hooks/APIs that NVDA currently uses. I highly doubt this could be implemented in Python (I could be wrong I'm no Python developer), it's likely this would have to be written in C++ and compiled to a form Python could access. This in itself would introduce more complexity than is needed for most cases, in my opinion, and could slow things down and cause more problems rather than being more helpful.

@tech10 JAWS introduced GDI hooking  as an alternative to its graphicks driver a few years ago. NVDA also uses GDI hooking. I am not suggesting that NVDA should start to use a graphics driver, as GDI hooking is just as good anyway, but instead to improve NVDA's GDI hooking Capabilities, so it gets as good as it is in JAWS

GDI hooking is a mechanism where screen readers load a dll into all other processes on the system, and api hooks a bunch of win32 functions that draw text to the screen, making them send information about the text, font and character locations back to the screen reader, before allowing the original function to run
I like this; it will be very helpful.
We would prefer to see this as an add-on as we expect this code will be very complex and challenging to maintain.

@gerald-hartig ok. That is fine for me. But for that to be as error free as possible, I think the display model code needs to be in its own separate dll, so add-ons can switch it out with their own dll without potentially causing issues to the other in process stuff

@Emil-18 
Do you plan to work on this?

@cary-rowen I am planning on making the API more suitable for add-ons to improve the display model, since NV Access didn't wanted an improved display model in the core. I am going to add a improved display model to my Enhanced Control Support add-on

> [@cary-rowen](https://github.com/cary-rowen) I am planning on making the API more suitable for add-ons to improve the display model, since NV Access didn't wanted an improved display model in the core. I am going to add a improved display model to my Enhanced Control Support add-on

@ruifontes You can find it here, as of now, without any improvements to the display model. I don't know how long it is going to take until I have the display model stuff ready. https://github.com/Emil-18/enhanced-control-support
Thanks!

body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Currently, it is possible to have the review cursor follow the mouse (set in Review Cursor settings).  This makes it possible for a low vision user to hover the mouse over objects (such as text on a web page) and use the review cursor to interact with it.

Having the system focus / caret follow the mouse would enable a low vision user to hover over an object or text and then interact with it in the same way as if they had used the keyboard to navigate there.

This would also make it easier for low vision users to become accustomed to the NVDA commands while they still use the mouse as it would be easier to use a combination of both approaches.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A setting in NVDA's mouse settings called "System focus follows mouse" (or similar) which works like the current review cursor setting, but moving the system focus as the mouse moves.

Like the review cursor setting, I would propose leaving this disabled by default, which would then have no impact on existing users.  Like the review cursor option, low vision users could set this as desired (and it would be enabled disabled with mouse tracking as other mouse options currently).

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Using the current review cursor following mouse functionality does work, however has a number of limitations, and the majority of users are less familiar with the review cursor in any case.

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body: There is an issue, #4896, requesting the ability to click and move focus & system caret in web pages.  The current suggested implementation for this is to implement it such that you would hold the NVDA key and click.  One of the main reasons I have proposed this issue is that the request we get from partially sighted users is, essentially, to be able to move the mouse over text (often on a web page), and press something to have NVDA read from that point.  With the #4896 implementation, you would need to hover the mouse over your text, hold NVDA and click, then press say all.  I think that is worth implementing as there is a logic to "I want to move focus here so I'm going to click here" - which is how the mouse often works.  This issue suggests a simpler "move the mouse and press one keystroke" solution which could work alongside that.
Closing in favour of #4896, implementing this would have a large amount of side effects making handling focus dangerous
Opening following team discussion re implementing this for Browse mode (for technical reasons)

@Qchristensen could you clarify the output of your internal discussion: what is the exact use case(s), the limitation(s), etc.

Would it only work for browse mode? Why is browse mode different from other contexts?

I would be very interested in this as a low vision user. The primary use case for me is in browse mode with cluttered web pages. In those cases, it can be easier to find a part of the page using magnification and set the browse mode cursor to that point. I wouldn't want the browse mode cursor to always follow the mouse - I'd rather have a single command to set the browse mode cursor to the element under the mouse and navigate from there. It looks like there was some discussion of doing a similar thing in #19539, but the feature I'd really be looking for is more general than "Say all". Rather than just doing Say All starting from the element under the mouse, it would be good to move the browse mode cursor to the mouse and then have the ability to navigate freely in browse mode from there. 
Is anyone currently working on this?

> I would be very interested in this as a low vision user. The primary use case for me is in browse mode with cluttered web pages. In those cases, it can be easier to find a part of the page using magnification and set the browse mode cursor to that point. I wouldn't want the browse mode cursor to always follow the mouse - I'd rather have a single command to set the browse mode cursor to the element under the mouse and navigate from there. It looks like there was some discussion of doing a similar thing in [#19539](https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/issues/19539), but the feature I'd really be looking for is more general than "Say all". Rather than just doing Say All starting from the element under the mouse, it would be good to move the browse mode cursor to the mouse and then have the ability to navigate freely in browse mode from there. Is anyone currently working on this?

I’m really looking forward to this. I’d even like to be able to use the mouse to read the contents of virtual documents. Unfortunately, I haven’t been able to achieve this yet.

This would likely require a lot of research into virtual documents and their TextInfo, which isn’t really my forte.

The current workaround is to use “sets the system caret to the position of the review cursor” to set the caret to a focusable position, such as a link, via the navigation object.

@hwf1324 do you have an idea who might be well versed in this topic? I saw in another thread that `NVDA+numpadMultiply` followed by `NVDA+numpadMinus` twice should work, but it hasn't worked consistently for me in Firefox or Chrome.

> [@hwf1324](https://github.com/hwf1324) do you have an idea who might be well versed in this topic? I saw in another thread that `NVDA+numpadMultiply` followed by `NVDA+numpadMinus` twice should work, but it hasn't worked consistently for me in Firefox or Chrome.

I'm not sure; it might require someone with a deep understanding of NVDA's underlying implementation.

Actually, what you're describing is the method I mentioned last, which is limited by the presence of focusable elements.

> [@Qchristensen](https://github.com/Qchristensen) could you clarify the output of your internal discussion: what is the exact use case(s), the limitation(s), etc.
> 
> Would it only work for browse mode? Why is browse mode different from other contexts?

Apologies for the delay!  I'll need to defer to @michaelDCurran for the technicalities - but my understanding is that implementing it for focus mode poses technically challenges browse mode would not for this.  The largest use case we get for this is reading text in browse mode like @RyanMcCleary's [comment above](#issuecomment-4166724075).  So I'm happy with that limitation for this - and if we do get a lot of requests from people who like it and want to expand it to focus mode as well AND find a way to implement that in the future, then great, we'll look at that in due course.

Meanwhile, for @RyanMcCleary issue #4896 requests "Click to move focus & system carat in web pages" which may do what you are after (once implemented)?

body:
### Steps to reproduce:
* Open the attached file "Bug sentence nav.docx"
* Press Alt+DownArrow many times to read the document sentence by sentence

### Actual behavior:
The first sentence is not read since the caret is already at the beginning of the first sentence.
When you press 3 times Alt+DownArrow, the following sentences are read:
* sentence 2
* sentence 2 + sentence 3
* sentence 4
The caret however moves correctly at the beginning of each sentence, but on the second press, NVDA reads the sentence preceding the caret and the sentence just following the caret.

### Expected behavior:
When you press 3 times Alt+DownArrow, the following sentences should be read:
* sentence 2
* sentence 3
* sentence 4
Since the caret moves correctly at the beginning of each sentence, on the second press, NVDA should read only the sentence just following the caret.

### Additional comment
The issue seems due to the 0x0B characters present between sentence 2 and 3. This is a situation that happens frequently in e-mails in Outlook. Maybe depending on the client used to write the e-mail?

### System configuration:

#### NVDA Installed/portable/running from source:
Installed

#### NVDA version:
2018.3.2

#### Windows version:
Version	10.0.17134 Numéro 17134

#### Name and version of other software in use when reproducing the issue:
Microsoft Word 2016, Microsoft Outlook 2016 (mail content)

#### Other information about your system:
N/A

### Other questions:

#### Does the issue still occur after restarting your PC?
Yes



[nvda.log](https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/files/2636992/nvda.log)
[Bug sentence nav.docx](https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/files/2636993/Bug.sentence.nav.docx)
[Bug sentence nav.docx](https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/files/2636995/Bug.sentence.nav.docx)
#### Have you tried any other versions of NVDA?
No

comments.body:
Adding attachments:
[Bug sentence nav.docx](https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/files/2636996/Bug.sentence.nav.docx)
[nvda.log](https://github.com/nvaccess/nvda/files/2636997/nvda.log)
Re-poening issue, sorry for closing it erronously.

This is happening because there is a soft return after sentence ```#2``` which actually is a new line character. This is not a real line break, it just tells MS Word to go to the next line. See also
https://word.tips.net/T000170_Understanding_Hard_and_Soft_Returns.html
You can reproduce this if you press shift enter after sentence ```#1```, then sentence ```#1``` and sentence ```#2``` will be spoken together when pressing alt+down arrow.
I think that NVDA's sentence rendering does not include soft line breaks yet, but if I am not wrong this should not be very difficult to fix.

@Adriani90 thanks for the link to the soft return, I did not understand how and why this character were inserted in some e-mails received by me.

To continue the analysis:
1. The issue does not occur when there is one soft return at the end of the sentence, but two at least.
2. It seems that the issue occurs when there is whichever combination of soft return (at least 2), spaces and no-break space between the previous sentence period and the next sentence first leter, and in whichever order.
3. The issue is due to a MS Word bug. Indeed you can see it using the native extension mode. After having issued a sentence move command, you can press F8 3 times to select the current sentence. However, if you move from sentence 2 to sentence 3 with Alt+downArrow and then press 3 times F8, you will see that Sentence 2 and Sentence 3 are selected instead of sentence 3 only.
Note: you need to press Escape to exit Word's extended selection mode.

@josephsl any thought on if it is useful to inform Microsoft of this issue, and if yes, how.

A solution to fix this issue would be to skip any space character (including soft and hard returns) after Word object model has returned the start position of the next sentence.
I already have a dirty try branch for this. If this solution is accepted, I can clean it and provide a PR for this.
cc: @mltony
I cannot reproduce this issue as of NVDA 2026.2 latest master (ee365d5a).

OK thanks. I have re-tested myself on 2025.3.2rc1. The issue is still present when using legacy COM object model, but not when using UIA.

It is quite strange since sentence navigation is still managed by legacy Word object model (until #19367 is merged).

@CyrilleB79 can you reproduce on alpha?
Can this be closed now or when #19367 is merged?

Either with NVDA 2025.3.2 or last alpha (alpha-53971,4a1e5c62 (2026.2.0.53971)): the bug is present with legacy mode, not with Word UIA mode.

Regarding #19367, I'll @codeofdusk test with his PR if it changes anything. But given the PR only applies to UIA mode, I do not expect so.

When testing this issue, double check opening `NVDA+f1` that Word is in IAccessible (legacy) mode. Indeed:
* when relaunching NVDA and before first focus change, Word is always in UIA mode no matter the configuration (see #13704 ).
* Not sure, but it seems that Word may not use IAccessible if the document is protected, e.g. if you open the document you have downloaded from internet (e.g. from this issue).

title: Windows 10: Start menu's search field does not always get focus

comments.body: The issue is that `getByRole` is fairly slow, but it's also syncronous, so `waitFor` keeps rerunning it and has to wait for each call to finish before other code can run. It could potentially use some performance improvements, but perhaps we could make it asyncronous (or add an alternative for back compatibility) so this can be run asyncronously.

Thanks for the quick reply Nick, does that not suggest that any `waitFor` that is sufficiently slow would trigger this? If so, is `waitFor` problematic in it's design?

What do you think the best short term resolution for this?
I'm guessing `waitFor` may be so eager to quickly rerun longer functions that it might actually be slowed down. You could try increasing the `interval` option, it defaults to only 50 milliseconds.

For reference the `getByRole` was taking 400-500ms and would cause the `waitFor` to loop 3-4 times. The best fix (for time) is to insert a more trivial query before that (like a `getByText`) as that flushes that promise much faster.

Would you like me to document some of this? Feels like a sufficiently frustrating morning that I don't want others to go through!

A higher interval might cause less loops. Even if it doesn't make this specific test run shorter, a longer interval could allow other tests to run in parallel more effectively. Can you try this out?

Alternatively, we could dynamically adjust the interval by default based on how long it takes each time. I'm not sure if that's worth the effort though.

Setting an interval of 750 still caused 2 iterations to fail (~450ms), then the fetch promise resolves. There's one more failure (output still not rendered to the screen), finally it passes.

Setting an interval underneath the `getByRole` time causes it to fail as expected. I.e. setting it to 200ms will cause 3 iterations and then the waitFor will just give up (total time is 1s by default if I remember).

I guess it works, but you keep having to up this arbitrary interval to "match" however long the waitFor will take as the complexity grows. That doesn't feel great to me.

Yea, I was thinking maybe `waitFor` could intelligently learn how long previous calls took and use that to adjust the interval. I'm not sure how well it would work in practice though. On the other hand, you may not need `*byRole` (see https://github.com/testing-library/dom-testing-library/issues/698#issuecomment-658714611), though PRs to improve performance would be welcome.

The problem is when the knowledge has been propogated differently;

https://kentcdodds.com/blog/common-mistakes-with-react-testing-library#using-the-wrong-query
https://testing-library.com/docs/guide-which-query/#priority

There's absolutely nothing in the docs about the downsides of `ByRole`, and previously I've been happy to accept the small performance hit for the better testing methodology. But this issue is straight out problematic now.

I would love to get an action on how best proceed with this, do we:

1. auto-increment `waitFor` intelligently
2. Change inner workings of `ByRole` to be asynchronous
3. Provide documentation around this known issue with suitable guidance

etc.

1 would be cool to experiment with but as I said previously, I'm not sure how difficult to implement and useful it would be, so it may not be worth it. I'm fine with either 2 or 3.

@kentcdodds @eps1lon What do you think about an asyncronous version of `*byRole` (2) to work around performance issues like this?

One question to make sure I understand. What is your test waiting on that takes more than a single 50ms interval? Most/All tests using this utility should mock out any async code so it resolves on the next tick or two of the event loop 🤔

Just noticed the screenshot, sorry about that. Looks like it's getting mocked by MSW, so I wouldn't expect this to take longer than a few milliseconds. Something is up there.

The `*ByRole` query is definitely the performance hog of the bunch, but it's a cost with the benefit of confidence which is why it's the top most recommended query. I suppose we could add a note to the docs warning about this. Probably a good idea. I'd love to make that query faster, but I still recommend it.

In this situation, there's something especially fishy going on. I think we'd need a reproduction to dive any deeper though.

@kentcdodds yer to confirm MSW is registering is ~1ms so it's very fast. It's the `*ByRole` that's exceeding the `waitFor` interval window.

Reproduction will take some time, is it not clear that exceeding the `waitFor` interval window will cause this?

If you want to optimize tests for performance go through these steps until you're satisfied. They're ordered by their confidence impact (lower impact, higher ranked) then perf impact (higher impact, higher ranked).
1. Make sure you use the latest version of `jsdom`
1. don't use JSDOM but modern browsers
1. `getByRole(role, { hidden: true })` (WARNING: includes inaccessible elements - avoids expensive visibility check)
1. `getByTestId` instead of `getByRole`
1. don't filter by `name` in `getByRole` (Can rely on order in `getAllByRole`. Order of elements in the DOM is in most cases not an implementation detail.)

Not using `jsdom` at all is the best solution since it has better perf and higher confidence.
@eps1lon great walkthrough 👍 though I think I'd prefer to try another query before testIDs. I think it makes sense to add this to the docs.

> though I think I'd prefer to try another query before testIDs. I think it makes sense to add this to the docs.

Definitely! It was easier to say since "use another query" might not be that helpful. If you're really blocked by perf problems I would just suggest a quick escape hatch and revisit it once the test breaks on unrelated changes (though that might be even harder to judge). It's just really hard to give actionable advise that people can apply to different scenarios.

I think the most generic advise is to re-evaluate your `waitFor` usage. If you're in a unit-test-like environment you should be able to mock the timers so that you can deterministically forward to the point where your assertion should be true (skipping the wasteful polling approach). In e2e tests (with real timers/requests) I'd like to see a reproduction since I would expect a browser to not have perf problems with `getByRole(role, { name })`.

And to give an optimistic outlook: The `ByRole` implementation is just a polyfill at the moment. Long term we'll use actual DOM APIs that are hopefully faster (see https://wicg.github.io/aom/spec/#computed-accessibility-tree).

I would definitely try item 3 from @eps1lon's checklist for this particular case - the visibility calculation is expensive in large DOM trees
```
getByRole(role, { hidden: true })
```

Thanks for the input here everyone. I think some docs here would go a long way.

@herecydev once you've tried and had success with one of these recommendations, would you mind working on improvement the documentation to help others who may face this in the future?

> I would definitely try item 3 from @eps1lon's checklist for this particular case - the visibility calculation is expensive in large DOM trees

To touch on another potential optimization point: In Material-UI we set the [default value](https://testing-library.com/docs/dom-testing-library/api-configuration#configuration) to `true` (fast option) in local development and to false (slow) in CI. The reason being that you rarely accidentally query for inaccessible elements. If you do, it should be obvious in the test which we'll check during code review (since an explicit `hidden` option stands out). In 1015 `ByRole` calls we only ever need to explicitly specify the `hidden` state in 25 cases. So in the vast majority of cases we just check the `hidden` state for confidence not because the test is actually about the `hidden` state.

The other part is that CI can be a little bit slower (since it already takes a couple of minutes). However, for local development you want snappy feedback so every millisecond counts.

That might be worth documenting. Though we shouldn't integrate that into the library since we don't know whether people have CI or if every commit passes through it.

My app is using Material UI and I was seeing about 6 seconds per query with `getByRole()` making my total test time about 20 seconds. After switching the queries to `getByLabelText()` the test time went down to 600ms - HUGE difference. The component under test is a Drawer with about 50 checkboxes in it.

I agree that a small performance cost is worth the confidence, but it becomes unusable when that cost is about 6 seconds. I definitely agree in having some documentation updates to give visibility into this tradeoff.

There's a place for filtering by `textContent` (e.g. `listitem` causes oftentimes confusion since people want to query them by their content but they're not named by content but author). The accessible name as well as accessibility tree inclusion requires CSS. Calculating styles is slow in JSDOM which is why you see these drastic gains outside of JSDOM.

That seems like an acceptable solution. Is there a reason to have it scheduled for 10 ms? Could we not do this and achieve the same goal just faster?

```ts

```

>Is there a reason to have it scheduled for 10 ms? Could we not do this and achieve the same goal just faster?

I'm not so sure, consider this very common case:
```js
const response = await fetch('api');
const json = await response.json();
```
the timeline would look something like this:
- `fetch()` is called in the main thread, it is awaited so the main thread is relinquished
- `setTimeout` immediately completes and queues its task
- `fetch()` resolves and queues its task
- the main thread is done
- the `setTimeout` task runs
- the `fetch()` task runs
- `response.json()` is called, main thread is relinquished again
- `setTimeout` immediately completes and queues its task
- `response.json()` resolves and queues its task
- the main thread is done
- the `setTimeout` task runs
- the `response.json()` task runs
- we finally get to render what we wanted

Some async operations depend on multiple tasks to run to completion to get what we want, but we run too much code in between.

Something similar actually happened to us with `react-focus-lock`, we had many `userEvent.tab()` before a `waitFor`, but `react-focus-lock` had many slow async tasks queued that we couldn't get past `response.json()` in time.

I've profiled issues with this in our codebase locally, and 90% of the speed issues are coming from checking to see if the element's subtree is "accessible", meaning if it's potentially visible to the user. The problem is it recursively checks every element, and worse, runs a `getComputedStyle` on it to see if its visible.

There's a new API for that now [checkVisibility](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Element/checkVisibility), but that's probably not going to help much in node.

It's probably prudent to add an escape hatch here to skip this check. something like `getAllByRole('option', { strictVisibilityCheck: false })`

Honestly, I think even having this check to begin with is a bit pedantic given the nature of these tests. If it was my project, I would default to not even checking that unless someone had it on `getAllByRole('option', { strictVisibilityCheck: true })` or the like.

@benlesh problem with `hidden: true` is that it does nothing for `description` or `name` options. If we have `name`, the code calls [computeAccessibleName](https://github.com/testing-library/dom-testing-library/blob/main/src/queries/role.ts#L273) which eventually calls [isHidden](https://github.com/eps1lon/dom-accessibility-api/blob/main/sources/accessible-name-and-description.ts#L71) which gets computed styles anyway and causes the slowdown. So while simple `*ByRole(role, { hidden: true })` gets speed up, using `*ByRole(role, { description, hidden: true })` or `*ByRole(role, { name, hidden: true })` is as slow as without `hidden`.

If we have `name`, the code calls [computeAccessibleName](https://github.com/testing-library/dom-testing-library/blob/main/src/queries/role.ts#L273) which eventually calls [isHidden](https://github.com/eps1lon/dom-accessibility-api/blob/main/sources/accessible-name-and-description.ts#L71) which gets computed styles anyway and causes the slowdown. So while simple `*ByRole(role, { hidden: true })` gets speed up, using `*ByRole(role, { description, hidden: true })` or `*ByRole(role, { name, hidden: true })` is as slow as without `hidden`.
